Hi @thechief389,

This project is about as old as the TOML spec itself. If you want to know why another, similar parse exists, you would have to ask the maintainer of that project. I think it is healthy for any spec to have multiple implementations, even in the same programming language.

I do not currently have any plans to abandon this project so long as downstream users keep reporting bugs and requesting features. My contributions to this project are made strictly on my own time. While I wish I had more free time to devote to it, that does not seem likely in the foreseeable future.

Seeing as this is neither a bug nor a feature request, I am closing this as WONTFIX.

If you have any feature requests or bugs to report, please open a new issue.

Thanks,
-Will

Well I like it. I like that you can easily "pop in" to a chunk of async IO code for efficient, interrupt driven IO, then "pop out" to continue with your sync code.

> a big can of worms.

Yeah, it's definitely a big can of worms! But it's unavoidable AFAICT. Some facts that are unlikely to change:

* async functions, and objects based on async functions like async generators, can be garbage collected.
* garbage collection happens in an environment where no coroutine runner is available.
* at garbage collection time, we have to perform clean up

Conclusion: async functions MUST have the capability to synchronously clean up after themselves.

I guess one could try to change those bullet points somehow, but it seems difficult. And AFAICT, once you have those, then the conclusion follows inevitably.

When I first read through curio I saw your comment with that rationale for `async def close`, and I too sort of shrugged and said "huh, yeah, that's a tricky one, I'm not sure what the right way is either". But now that I've bumped into this cleanup issue, I think that tips the balance :-). For something like socket close that can and always will be synchronous, I think the right solution is just to make `close` synchronous and to *only* support `__enter__`/`__exit__` (not `__aenter__`/`__aexit__`), in order to avoid confusion (like you say).

The broader/trickier question is indeed what to do with types like your async file with buffering, or async generators, where there are legitimate reasons why you'd like to be able to do async I/O during cleanup. In these cases you *must* have *some* strategy available for doing synchronous cleanup (as per the argument above), but it would kinda suck if we made that the only option available... yet we don't want to trick users into calling the wrong one.

One idea: for these cases, create the convention that you get methods:

* `async def aclose(self)`: does a clean shutdown
* `def synchronous_close(self)`: emits a SynchronousCleanupWarning and does a violent shutdown (e.g. discarding buffers and closing sockets, like how `file.close` apparently works according to your bugs.python.org/issue25476 link)
* `__del__`: calls `synchronous_close`
* `__enter__` or `__exit__`/`close`: undefined, to avoid confusion
* `__aenter__` / `__aexit__`: defined, and `__aexit__` looks like:

```
async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb):
    if exc_type is GeneratorExit:
        # Must be handled synchronously
        self.synchronous_close()
    else:
        await self.aclose()
```

So the net effect of all of the above would be that user code can just do `async with ...:`, and if the user code runs to completion it will DTRT. But if users don't use `async with ...:`, or they do but their stack gets garbage collected before completing (i.e. *their* caller doesn't clean up properly), then we still do get *a* clean up, just a sub-optimal one with a warning.

And I guess `aclose` on async generators would have to inject a new built-in exception type, like

```
async def aclose(self):
    if not self.gi_running:
        # if we haven't started, nothing to do
        return
    try:
        await self.athrow(AsyncGeneratorExit)
    except AsyncGeneratorExit:
        pass
    except:
        raise
    else:
        raise RuntimeError('async generator ignored AsyncGeneratorExit')
```

It mighhht even make sense to do something like this for async functions, not just async generators... not sure.

@cmacmackin 

Dear Chris,

in the just uploaded master branch I have added this feature. I have added 2 dedicated tests, [one](https://github.com/szaghi/FoBiS/tree/master/src/unittest/python/install-test1) for executables and [another](https://github.com/szaghi/FoBiS/tree/master/src/unittest/python/install-test2) for libraries.

The new `install` command works essentially as you indicated, but with some differences:

+ the prefix specification must be *comprehensive*, namely FoBiS does not add `bin` or `lib` sub-path automatically; this can be changed easily in the future, but for the moment I prefer to adopt a minimal footprint *a priori* assumptions (I am not sure that `bin` or `lib` are always adopted);
+ for the installation of libraries I currently copy only the (possible) module associated to the `target` source file: for building a library with FoBiS you always must specify a target source, thus it comes very natural to copy its module file in the case it is a module; in the future adding options to copy others modules or excluding other ones should be very easy;
+ I do not change the *compiling heritage* mechanism, rather I added a new hidden file category: `*.track_build* files are created (into the build directory) for each built object (for all targets either automatic targets or user specified); into each `*.track_build` file there are the infos necessary for the subsequent installation.

Due to my choice on how *track* buildings, you have to respect the order

```shell
FoBiS.py build
FoBiS.py install --prefix path_of_your_choice
```

If you try to install without a *build structure* you obtain the following error:

```shell
→ FoBiS.py install -p there
Error: build directory not found! Maybe you have to run "FoBiS.py build" before.
```

Before making this master branch a new release I would like to have to your feedback about all the modifications I made today.

Cheers

I added a subdivide_to_size method, which subdivides triangles until every edge is shorter than a specified length, which you can use for (better than the ray method anyway) voxelization:

```
    pitch=0.5
    max_edge = pitch / 2.0

    # get the same mesh sudivided so every edge is shorter than half our pitch              
    v,f = trimesh.remesh.subdivide_to_size(m.vertices, m.faces, max_edge)

    # convert the vertices to their voxel grid position                                     
    hit = (v / pitch)
    # get a conservative-ish wrapping                                                       
    hit = np.vstack((np.ceil(hit), np.floor(hit))).astype(int)

    # remove duplicates                                                                     
    u,i = trimesh.grouping.unique_rows(hit)

    # get the voxel centers in model space                                                  
    occupied = hit[u]*pitch

    m.visual.face_colors = [255,0,0,100]

    vox = trimesh.voxel.multibox(occupied, pitch)

    (vox+m).show()

```

I'm pretty sure that it is. There is the `/gallery/folders` and `/collections/folders` API endpoint and the output of both of them always correlate with what you see on the corresponding "Gallery" and "Favourites" pages on DeviantArt itself. It is also impossible to use a gallery-folder ID as a collection ID and vice versa, so both of them are handled as separate entities.

> I should have been more clear with my question, I was more concerned about really getting all possible items to be honest.

The GalleryExtractor using `gallery/all` definitively gets all images in a user's gallery (your previous tests pretty much confirmed that, didn't they), but going through all gallery-folders surprisingly doesn't give the same result:

```bash
# using gallery/all
$ gallery-dl -g http://rosuuri.deviantart.com/ | sort | uniq | wc -l
386
# going through all gallery-folders
$ gallery-dl -o flat=false -gg http://rosuuri.deviantart.com/ | sort | uniq | wc -l
366
```

There are for some reason 20 images missing, even though the total amount of returned URLs is ca. 750. Using `flat=false` might not be the ideal way - currently. That sounds like an interesting proposition, but you can do something like this manually:
- group your input file according to the options they need
- write a shell-/batch-script that calls gallery-dl multiple times for each input-file/options combination
```
gallery-dl -i files01 -c config01.cfg
gallery-dl -i files02 -c config02.cfg
...
```

Okay, more debugging.... looking in the IMM code there are two divisions, the divide by cbar, and then the normalization of mu. Turns out the latter generates nan, which then propagates into P in subsequent iterations.


```python
        self.mu =  self.cbar * L
        self.mu /= sum(self.mu) # normalize
```
L is the likelihood of each filter. On inspection it is 0 for both. So essentially the filter has diverged so far that the math for the likelihood (which is the probability that the measurement fits the filter) ends up being 0. I'm sure this is a finite precision issue - it is probably 10^343234 or some ridiculously small number. 

If I add this code after bank.update() my filter runs without issue (again, with my data, not yours):
```python
    if not np.isscalar(bank.mu):
        bank.mu = np.array([0.5, 0.5])
```

I'm continuing to try to understand the AST. I wrote a node visitor that collects the set of object types that appear for each attribute of each ast node type. Here are a few pecularities I observed, when I extract the statistics for every parse happening during `test_c_parser.py`:

- `ID`: Appears mostly as a reference to existing name, most of the time acting as an expression. However, it also appears in `field` of `StructRef`, where it doesn't really represent an expression on it's own, but at least a reference to an existing name. Furthermore, it appears in the `name` of other `ID`'s, which sounds like a bug to me.

- `Decl`/`TypeDecl`: As described in my previous comment, it appears that there are two tiers of type describing nodes: The first tier is `ArrayDecl`,`PtrDecl`,`FuncDecl`, which can be nested among each other and may additionally contain `TypeDecl`. `TypeDecl` acts as gateway to the second tier, consisting of `Enum`, `Union`, `Struct` and `IdentifierType`. However, the `type` attribute of `Decl` doesn't seem to conform to that separation, it may contain nodes of either tier. It appears to me that `Decl` is possibly covering multiple distinct concepts, which leads to an unclear meaning of that node.

I think this issue shouldn't be closed. *xdot* needs to be able to handle valid DOT files, its own purpose and main goal is explicitly that one. If the main supporting library has a bug that prevents the expected correct behavior, this could (should?) be considered a bug in xdot itself (regardless where is the responsibility of the fix).

At a minimum, keeping this issue opened as a way of tracking progress on the fix would be a good practice; some noise in the Dot project bugtracker would also be helpful. All it was needed so far was a "me too" message in [their bug report](https://gitlab.com/graphviz/graphviz/issues/165) and it got noticed pretty quickly by the devs.

For a workaround to this issue, I've been using this simple *sed* command that cleans up the GStreamer DOT files:

```
sed -i -e 's/\\\\//g' pipeline.dot
```

It is not related to any version of Python but to Synology products.
Python assumes by default that if nothing is specified (as it is the case on Synology products) then the file system encoding is ascii.

This is WRONG. To run Python on a Syno, you have to patch it at compilation. Optware doesn't use such a patch and thus still have the bug.
Superzebulon's SPKs uses my patch for that but doesn't include setuptools nor pip and this prevents you from installing/upgrading python modules.

I will give you a patched Python in a SPK with setuptools and pip tonight. What's your arch?

My test setup was bad, so I didn't catch this error. The problem is that the question mark returns a different group even though it matches the same elements. For example`d.*j.*m.*i` will return the group 'django_mi', while `d.*?j.*?m.*?i` will return the group 'django_migrati'. This throws off the ranking algorithm. I'm sure you knew this already, but I certainly learned something :)

On the positive side of my test setup being bad; the timing is actually radically different. A 5x speed improvement without the `?` on my test data. Assuming relatively few elements in the list would match, it would be possible to do a second pass with the more expensive regex in case of a match, but probably not worth the readability impact.

There is a couple of undocumented features/regressions I wanted to discuss:

1. The documentation says that one of the design choices was that "all options must be settable via command line". But specifying `default_config_files` in the `ArgumentParser` constructor makes it possible to have a config file path that is not settable via command line.

    I'd suggest to hold the design choice and solve this with dropping the constructor parameters and rely only on the `add_argument()` method, see next points for details.

2. Currently all default config files are parsed and values from user-specified files are placed on top. I find this behaviour rather unpractical for a library, because this way values from the default config files cannot be unset by the user. An alternative is to read only one default config file (the last one), and when the user specified an alternative config file path, don't read default configs at all. This is loosely coupled with 1.

3. It is theoretically possible to set default config path per-argument in the `add_argument()` method, for example:

    ``` python
    parser.add_argument("-c", "--config", default=["foo.conf", "bar.conf"],  is_config_file_arg=True, help="config file path")
    ```

    IMO this is more flexible than specifying everything in the constructor, but it is not supported by ConfigArgParse yet (at least the default config files specified this way are handled differently from those specified in the constructor).

I have a prototype addressing the above issues in my [config_files_without_merging](https://github.com/lahwaacz/ConfigArgParse/compare/master...lahwaacz:config_files_without_merging) branch, but since it breaks stuff (including some tests), it should be discussed before merging into master.

    import sys

    _PY2 = (sys.version_info < (3, 0))

    def identity(x):

    # u('string') replaces the forwards-incompatible u'string'
    if _PY2 and not _IRONPYTHON:
        import codecs
        
        def u(string):
            return codecs.unicode_escape_decode(string)[0]
    else:
        u = identity

    # dict.iteritems(), dict.iterkeys() is also incompatible
    if _PY2:
        iteritems = dict.iteritems
        iterkeys = dict.iterkeys
    else:

Those are the two options and I put a bit of thought into what would make
sense I'm terms of separation of responsibilities. In the end the structure
you have would make sense as mixins, not as layers of multiple inheritance.

The problem with having things like `IDF_file_io`, `IDF_object_handler`,
etc. as subclasses of `IDF` is that then you have to change everywhere you
refer to IDF to refer to one of those instead.

The argument against mixins is less clearcut. In favour: it might help to
have the various types of functions clearly separated and in class with
useful names. Against: it's a less clear style of programming, and they
would depend so closely on `IDF` that there is little chance they'd be used
anywhere else.

If you want to take a look at `IDF` in a single class (with documentation),
have a look in my repo. My feeling is that's the right way to go, supported
by comprehensive unit tests.

Hey @tobes, thanks for the feedback :)

> It would be fantastic if the functionality could be added into the existing xrandr module but looking at it I think that would be a tight squeeze so maybe it's much better to have them separate

I also initially thought of extending existing xrandr, but I kind of want to control monitor layout and monitor rotation separately, thus I ended up with a small separate module.

> I'm sure I can 'magically' get you the current display from the xrandr module (if it is running) that would solve the multi-monitor issue.

Not really, or I don't fully understand the suggestion. I can retrieve all currently used screens from xrandr (execute `xrandr` and look where `*` sign is), but if people have 2 screen setup where one screen extends another, do you think we should rotate *all of them* at the same time? If I completely misunderstood you, could you give me an example? :)

> Instead of the {icon} I'd be inclined to just have a format_horizontal and format_vertical or maybe format_h and format_v.

I think having `format` option with possible placeholders is kind of standard and obvious way. There is also an attempt to bring old modules to such approach (see #98). That's the reason I created a `format` with the `{icon}` placeholder.

In most cases I think nobody would extend the `format` itself, but will specify `horizontal_icon` and `vertical_icon` - if using FontAwesome, for example.

> Although it might be nice to spin the display all the way normal > 90 left > 180 > 90 right is there a reason for limiting this?

The reason is simplicity, you won't do it every day. As I see the usage scenario, you have a screen that you have normally in horizontal position, and when sometimes you want to read a long text, you rotate it vertically. You won't rotate it every time differently though, it would be a habit - either clockwise, or counter-clockwise. Therefore I think in everyday usage you would require only two options.

I'm very much annoyed by `xrandr` module asking me options that I never use (I always switch between 2 out of 4 available), so I didn't want to be annoyed by "too many options" in this module as well :)



Let me know what you think!

@tillahoffmann Thanks for the changes! I'd like to come back to those later, I think first we should discuss the main issue some more.

As it has happened before, we've reached a crossroads where a decision has to be made, but all the available options have some disadvantages.
I think it would be good to consider the options we are currently talking about, and also think about additional options that might come to mind.

I'm listing the options I can think of, probably there are even more?
Once we know all options, we can try to find an agreement on how to proceed.

**Option 0, keep the status quo**

This behaves correctly (AFAICT), but it is quite inefficient in the overlap case.
No `overlap` support for non-seekable files.

**Option 1, the original proposal of this PR**

Makes all investigated cases faster and adds `overlap` support for non-seekable files, but introduces a bug.
I'm now quite certain that it actually *is* a bug, and that a sentence in the documentation doesn't make that acceptable.
It is actually not as unlikely as I initially thought, that a user will want to modify the given `out` parameter.
A prime example would be applying a window in-place, but there is range of other signal processing steps that a user could want to apply as in-place operations. They might even use the array temporarily for something completely different.
Relying on the user not modifying `out` between iterations would be a violation of the principle of least surprise (regardless whether it is mentioned in the docs or not).

**Option 2, modify the current PR to use a separate array for storing the overlap if `out` is given**

This should get rid of the bug, but it might also reduce the performance win.
And it makes the implementation more complicated.
It is also a violation of the principle of least surprise because if I provide an `out` parameter, I don't expect the implementation to allocate additional memory.

**Option 3, disable `overlap` when `out` is given**

I don't know if anybody would ever want to use both arguments at the same time, but theoretically, this would be a breaking change.
But it would be quite a simple solution to get rid of the bug and still get all the performance benefits.

**Option 4, fall back to using `seek()` when `out` is given**

I guess this would also get rid of the bug, but it will make the implementation uglier.

**Option 5, get rid of `overlap` altogether, without replacement**

Doesn't require `seek()`, simpler implementation, we can choose the fastest implementation.
But the users have to do some of the work on their own.

When we were discussing the implementation of `blocks()` in #35, the `overlap` argument (and several variations thereof) was a large part of the discussion.
This leads me to believe that this functionality isn't at all obvious or uncontroversial.
What about getting rid of it and letting users implement their own overlapping schemes if needed?
@tillahoffmann What would it take in your case to implement the overlapping in your own code?
You could reserve (and zero-initialize) an array of size `blocksize + overlap` and then use a `blocksize`-sized slice at the end of it as `out` argument to `blocks()`. Once you are done with whatever processing you want to do, you can copy the last `blocksize` frames from the end of your array to the beginning and continue iteration.
That doesn't sound too hard, does it?
And the advantage is that somebody with a different use case can implement a different overlapping scheme that's more appropriate in their case.

I think we could argue with the same arguments as in #205, which might be a similar situation.

BTW, the same reasoning could be applied to @bastibe's question whether we should internally read larger blocks from the file and then split them into pieces before yielding them to the user. I think the users should choose what block size they want to use for *reading* from the file (that's what the `soundfile` library is for, isn't it?), but if they need smaller pieces later, they should to the splitting themselves (and they can optimize it much better for their specific use case!).
They could of course also implement their own file-like object that does some optimized buffering.
Either way should be possible to implement with the `soundfile` module, but IMHO the implementation itself is out-of-scope for the `soundfile` module.

The values are used to give ETAs of when an initial blockchain sync will complete.  As the chain is always extending, and processing time is more a function of tx count than block count, I wanted a way to give a sensible estimate.  So TX_COUNT_HEIGHT is just any recent height (10,000 blocks back is fine), TX_COUNT is the count at that height, and TX_PER_BLOCK is a guess at how many txs, on average, subsequent blocks will have.

There is no right answer; it's just used for giving the user feedback on how long they have to wait.  It doesn't work well on bitcoin testnet though as testnet is insanely lumpy - many blocks with few txs, and then several hundred blocks that are swamped.

Sorry for the slow reply on this. One may be able to put together a wrapper which would be smart enough to behave the right way even when applied to a decorator, but there are a few issues around this. It isn't something I think I could sensibly do as part of the wrapt decorator default wrapper because I don't know how people would use it, but since your ``remove()`` function hides the fact that ``wrapt.decorator`` is used and you only use its basic functionality, you could possibly replace use of ``wrapt.decorator`` such that a derived version of ``FunctionWrapper`` is used which adds descriptor support.

The things to consider are as follows.

When the decorator is placed inside of ``@property``, the wrapper would only be created once. If placed outside and give descriptor functionality, it is likely the ``FunctionWrapper`` instance would need to be created on each access of the property, adding the cost of the wrapper creation.

You have to deal with ``setter`` and ``deleter`` being used on the ``getter`` property, and thus the wrapper, to add functions for those as well. If the ``getter`` is marked as removed, then presumably these are also marked as removal.

Thinking about it, it may all up not be too bad. The ``remove()`` function when given the thing to be wrapped can do an ``isinstance()`` check for type ``property`` and totally adjust how it works and rather than use a decorator, use an ``ObjectProxy`` which overrides the parts of property to allow interception of access to add the decorator on each access.

APScheduler is pretty strict about being punctual so its default misfire grace time is set to 1 second. If for any reason the execution is delayed beyond that, the execution is skipped as a misfire. To avoid that you can set the `misfire_grace_time` option to a higher value either on the scheduler level or individual job level.

In the future, please only report actual bugs on this bug tracker. Ask questions on the Google group or StackOverflow.

I'd say choosing between bytes/text based on existence of content-length (which is a SHOULD for all SEND/MESSAGE/ERROR frames that have a payload in the STOMP spec, and a MUST if it contains null octets) is a fragile heuristic on a header that's not intended for that purpose, and nothing that helps with "robust interaction". > can "wormhole" keep local state about whether the user used "tab" in any of the last 10 times they've used "wormhole rx"? you could choose a different prompt based on whether they're regular tab users.

Not yet.. we don't yet create `~/.wormhole/`, which is where that stuff wants to live. In the future, when we add a state directory for other reasons, that'd be a great place to remember it.

> fwiw, i'd advocate updating the prompt well before hitting return -- if
it's possible to update the prompt as soon as libreadline knows it has
something "useful" to tab-complete, that would be ideal :)

Well, that's the question :). It's useful (and common) to hit Tab before you type anything else, because if there's only one channel allocated at that moment, it will complete on that, so you won't have to type any digits at all (it will fill in the hyphen too). If you like, you can hit Tab before typing any letters either, and you'll get the full wordlist to choose from, which is a bit on the large side (256 words) but you can almost just visually search for the right one, which could help e.g. if you didn't read english at all (straight pattern-matching).

So on any given execution of the program, it's useful to remind the user about tabs from the very beginning. But if they already know about tabs, then any reminder is just cluttering up the screen.

The hybrid approach is to mention tabs after the fact, which doesn't help anyone the first time through, but does provide a discovery path so that *next* time they might find things easier. And by that point we know whether they used tabs this time or not, so we can avoid saying anything if they're already aware of the feature.

But then the question is where to place this notification. It can't be before the prompt because they might already know about tabs and (without saved state) we can't know that yet. We can either put it inside the prompt (which would be less cluttersome, but we don't know how to make libreadline do that), or on the line after the prompt (which is slightly cluttersome).

I guess I'm leaning towards the post-prompt line, although maybe adding a few spaces would help distinguish it from any `--text=` message that follows.

0) Protocols use std ports since ever. Unless security is a concern user should be permitted to either use a standard port and reduce typing load or use a random port and transmit it. SSH faced similar problems, when in fact stakes where much higher, a rooted machine vs 1 file.
1) Base36 is indeed easier to transmit verbally. Again user could choose what to use. At library side, library could calculated both. At receive side typing either, can be easily detected. 
2) Maybe sender/receiver could opt for 1-time passwords, or a pre-shared key/secret knock
3) ipv6 is a tough nut. 128 bits ~ 25 chars/base36. Static Ip & caching could help.  Maybe static-ip are issued by default in a ipv6 world. Cheers!

So I guess the way I think about `bind` is that it's amortizing the cost of sending `appid` and `side` with every subsequent message: if we wanted to enable sharing a single websocket connection between multiple clients in the same process (probably sending to distinct recipients), then we'd add `appid` and `side` in open/list/allocate/add/etc, and remove `bind` entirely. I don't want to enable that yet, but the protocol ought to behave the same either way. That's why I don't think `bind` needs a response.

That said, I also have in mind a size-limiting scheme for non-file-transfer applications (a Petmail addressbook+key -exchange is my typical example) where the clients might want to send more data than normal, and there needs to be some sort of negotiation between client and server about how much can be sent (how many messages, maximum size, how long they persist without at least one client keeping them alive, how long the mailbox can last despite keepalives). If the client asks for more than the server is willing to offer, there needs to be a response. The client's request could go into the `bind` along with the `appid`, and the server could send an `error` if the client asks for too much. Or the size/age request could go into the `open` or `allocate` instead.

So I still think that means `bind` doesn't need a response, but I'll admit it isn't completely clear-cut.

There are a couple of options, all of which are out of scope of django-jsonview:

* I usually add a `MyModel.to_dict()` method, which lets me be _very_ explicit about which fields are returned, and any transformations (like printing dates in ISO8601). Then I return `obj.to_dict()` instead of just `obj`.
* If all the attributes are JSON serializable (by default jsonview uses the Django JSON encoder which can handle datetime objects, too) you could, in theory, just return `obj.__dict__` (though I don't recommend that).
* You could write a [custom JSON encoder](https://docs.python.org/2/library/json.html#json.JSONEncoder) that handles your types, and [use that encoder](https://github.com/jsocol/django-jsonview#configuring-json-output).

SOLVED:

You just make a file like this one:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
  <dict>
    <key>Label</key>
    <string>com.butterfly.app</string>
    <key>ProgramArguments</key>
    <array>
      <string>/usr/bin/python</string>
      <string>/usr/local/bin/butterfly.server.py</string>
      <string>--unsecure</string>
    </array>
    <key>KeepAlive</key>
    <true/>
    <key>RunAtLoad</key>
    <true/>
  </dict>
</plist>
```

and save it as com.butterfly.app.plist in ~/Library/LaunchAgents

Then in your shell, type
```sh
launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.example.app.plist
```
You might need to change the path in your Program Arguments.

```sh
      <string>/usr/bin/python</string> #Your path to python
      <string>/usr/local/bin/butterfly.server.py</string> #Your path to butterfly.server.py
```

Just wanted to follow up in case it can help someone in the future:

Using `ps -ef | grep python` showed me that there were in fact two copies of my script running.

My problem came from the fact that I am using [Flask](http://flask.pocoo.org/) and was running in debug mode. Flask includes reloading functionality such that you can change code and you don't need to restart your app. It's handy. Well, it accomplishes this by basically running two instances of your script. This is what was causing the flashing that I saw (two scripts writing to the matrix display that weren't perfectly in sync).

You can work around this issue in debug mode by adding `use_reloader=False` to your app.run() call. That fixed it for me. You lose the reloading functionality though. I believe switching out of debug mode should also solve it.

Anyway, problem solved. Thanks again @rm-hull 

[More info can be found here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/25504149/why-does-running-the-flask-dev-server-run-itself-twice)

    """

    """

    def run(self):
        """
        Outfiles are the libraries that were built using cmake
        """

        # There seems to be no other way to do this; I tried listing the

        # help would be appriciated

        self.outfiles = self.distribution.data_files

class InstallCMakeLibs(install_lib):
    """
    Get the libraries from the parent distribution, use those as the outfiles

    Skip building anything; everything is already built, forward libraries to
    the installation step
    """

    def run(self):
        """
        Copy libraries from the bin directory and place them as appropriate
        """

        self.announce("Moving library files", level=3)

        # We have already built the libraries in the previous build_ext step

        self.skip_build = True

        bin_dir = self.distribution.bin_dir

        libs = [os.path.join(bin_dir, _lib) for _lib in 
                os.listdir(bin_dir) if 
                os.path.isfile(os.path.join(bin_dir, _lib)) and 
                os.path.splitext(_lib)[1] in [".dll", ".so"]
                and not (_lib.startswith("python") or _lib.startswith("bpy"))]

        for lib in libs:

            shutil.move(lib, os.path.join(self.build_dir,
                                          os.path.basename(lib)))

        # Mark the libs for installation, adding them to 
        # distribution.data_files seems to ensure that setuptools' record 
        # writer appends them to installed-files.txt in the package's egg-info
        #
        # Also tried adding the libraries to the distribution.libraries list, 
        # but that never seemed to add them to the installed-files.txt in the

        # into eager_resources in the call to setup(), which I think puts them 
        # in data_files anyways. 
        #

        self.distribution.data_files = [os.path.join(self.install_dir, 
                                                     os.path.basename(lib))
                                        for lib in libs]

        self.distribution.run_command("install_data")

        super().run()

class InstallBlenderScripts(install_scripts):
    """
    Install the scripts available from the "version folder" in the build dir
    """

    def run(self):
        """
        Copy the required directory to the build directory and super().run()
        """

        self.announce("Moving scripts files", level=3)

        self.skip_build = True

        bin_dir = self.distribution.bin_dir

        scripts_dirs = [os.path.join(bin_dir, _dir) for _dir in

        for scripts_dir in scripts_dirs:

                os.path.splitext(_lib)[1] in [".dll", ".so"]

@jatinkrmalik There's a million ways to do this and there isn't a single right answer. 

Here are some approaches you could use:

1. Simplest: If you have a separate folder of images for each known user, you could call `compare_faces(known_images_for_one_user, unknown_image)` once for each folder of known images. If any of the results are `True` for a single call are true, then you know the face matches that known person.
2. More complex: You could build a classifier with scikit-learn that predicts a user name for a face encoding. The training data would be all the face encodings from all your known images (and the associated user name). You could use a support vector classifier (SVC), for example. The downside of this approach is that you have to re-train the classifier when you add more known pictures and that it will always try to say an unknown picture is one of your known users.
3. More complex / Scaling to larger data sets: You could use a clustering algorithm to put all the known face encodings into clusters and test an unknown user by seeing which cluster point it's closest to (and then verifying it using `compare_faces` but only against the faces in that cluster. I've been meaning to write an example program doing this, maybe using Spotify's [Annoy library](https://github.com/spotify/annoy) but I haven't had time yet.

Those are just some ideas. The first one would probably work fine for what it sounds like you want to do.

First, this line:

```python
                self.pool.map(self.threadComparison, [buff,names,enc])
```

doesn't really make any sense. You are sending `buff` to one thread, `names` to the next thread, and `enc` (which is never defined any may be picking up something defined in global scope) to the next thread. 

I'm guessing you might be trying to do something like where you send one element of each array together as three parameters to `self.threadComparison`?

```python
    function_parameters = zip(
        buff,
        names,
        enc
    )

    pool.starmap(self.threadComparison, function_parameters)
```

... but I could be wrong. But what you are currently doing doesn't make sense to me.

Next, I'm not really sure what the `self.threadComparison` function is exactly doing by checking hard-coded fixed indexes, but this line in particular is a bad idea:

```python
            self.found.append(vals[1][val])
```

When you call `pool.map`, Python will spin up multiple copies of the running code in different python instances. So each copy of the function will be running in a separate thread at that point. You won't be able to just write back to `self` there since there will be a different copy of `self` in each thread. You'll be saving the results inside just the copy of the object that exists in a temporary thread.

Instead, you need to return the result for one single lookup from each call to `self.threadComparison` (right now, nothing is being returned). As long as you return a result, the `pool.map()` will take care of combining all the results from all the different threads and giving you back one single combined list.

Next, you'll want to assign the result of the `pool.map()` function to a variable so that you can check the results. Right now, they are all just being thrown away.

So that doesn't exactly answer your question, but your code right now doesn't "work" in a conceptual sense, so I'm guessing the error is the side effect of some bad global data getting passed in the `enc` variable (or something). But first try fixing the code's basic logic and see if that doesn't take care of the error.

If that still doesn't fix it, you need to figure out what bad value is getting passed in as part of the second parameter to the pool.map() function.

Hope that helps! :)

It is sort-of possible, if not necessarily easy/straight-forward.

When you initialize the Security extension instance, you need to pass `register_blueprint=False`. Next, you need to create your own blueprint (named the same as what Flask-Security expects, aka `SECURITY_BLUEPRINT_NAME`, and register your routes/views with it: 
```
security = Blueprint('security', __name__, template_folder='templates')

# if you want to be able to load templates from flask_security, then you'll need a second (there can only be one template folder per blueprint):
flask_security = Blueprint('flask_security', 'flask_security', template_folder='templates')
```

Now, you use your own `security` blueprint to register views/routes: your custom view functions must use the same endpoint name as what Flask-Security expects, otherwise the 'url_for_security' stuff will break. Anywhere you don't need to customize anything, just use the default upstream view function when you're registering routes. The upstream defaults can be found [here](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/views.py#L337-L387)

Hope that helps!

The response object is the thing that is returned to the client and tells it to set the cookies. You are creating two separate response objects and not returning the one that does the setting of the cookies:

```python
    resp = jsonify({'login': True})
    set_access_cookies(resp, access_token)
    set_refresh_cookies(resp, refresh_token)
    resp = jsonify({'access_token':access_token,'refesh_token':refresh_token})
    return resp, 200
```

You should probably get rid of that second response object (you generally don't need to include your tokens in both the cookies and JSON data, but I don't know your application, maybe you have a valid use case for that). Alternately you can combine it with the first one like so.

```python
    resp = jsonify({
        'login': True,
        'access_token': access_token, 
        'refesh_token': refresh_token
    })
```

If you want to change the name of the that contains the access token, you can do that with ```app.config['JWT_ACCESS_COOKIE_NAME'] = 'access_token'```. 

Finally, if you are going to be using cookies, make sure to read up on how to prevent yourself from being vulnerable to CSRF attacks here: http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tokens_in_cookies.html

Cheers.

@MFlyer to start a background thread you can use `socketio.start_background_task()` function. That works as you describe, the function checks the async mode in use and then calls the appropriate function to spawn the thread or greenlet. There is also `socketio.sleep()` which works in the same way.

Killing threads though, is a bad practice. It isn't supported on some platforms, and in general is not a good idea because it may leak resources. So I think this should be left to the application, there is no standard way to stop a thread other than signaling the thread to end, and this signaling can be done in a number of different ways that are application specific.

According to my test, we have better performance now. 
Surely, placing ```__getattr__ = __getitem__ ``` in Row will have its impact. Before proposing the new implementation of Row I didn't know you could access an element of _extra _directly_.
As written in https://github.com/web2py/pydal/issues/246#issuecomment-118835089, my proposal is to change the current approach: writing ```db(db.tt).select('vv')``` should output  ```<Row {'vv': 1L}> ``` which is the output of  ```db(db.tt).select(db.tt.vv) ``` . Now we have  ```<Row {'_extra': {'vv': 1}}> ```. By doing that, we can avoid to add ```__getattr__ = __getitem__ ``` in Row.
I don't see why we should have two different results for the same select.

I am not aware of any changes in batch_dot, so it probably is still constrained to 3d input (first dim being batch_size).

We can write it out to check. The only value which changes is the tasks for which to change state at the end. You can keep the explicit methods as wrapper, but I still think we should maintain the shared logic in a single place.

```python

@provide_session
def set_dag_run_state(dag, execution_date, to_state, commit=False, session=None):
    """
    """
    res = []

    if not dag or not execution_date:
        return res

    # Mark the dag run to the new state.
    if commit:
        DR = DagRun
        dr = session.query(DR).filter(
            DR.dag_id == dag.dag_id,
            DR.execution_date == execution_date
        ).one()
        dr.state = to_state
        dr.end_date = timezone.utcnow()
        session.commit()

    # Mark all task instances of the dag run to the new state.
    if to_state != State.FAILED:
        tasks_to_change = dag.tasks
    # Only set running to failed.
    else:
        TI = TaskInstance
        task_ids = [task.task_id for task in dag.tasks]
        tis = session.query(TI).filter(
            TI.dag_id == dag.dag_id,
            TI.execution_date == execution_date,
            TI.task_id.in_(task_ids)).filter(TI.state == State.RUNNING)
        task_ids_of_running_tis = set([ti.task_id for ti in tis])
        tasks_to_change = [
            task for task in dag.tasks 
            if task.task_id in task_ids_of_running_tis
        ]
    for task in tasks_to_change:
        task.dag = dag
        new_state = set_state(task=task, execution_date=execution_date,
                              state=to_state, commit=commit)
        res.extend(new_state)

    return res
    

def set_dag_run_state_to_success(dag, execution_date, commit=False, session=None):
    set_dag_run_state(dag, execution_date, to_state=State.SUCCESS, commit=commit, session=session)

def set_dag_run_state_to_failed(dag, execution_date, commit=False, session=None):
    set_dag_run_state(dag, execution_date, to_state=State.FAILED, commit=commit, session=session)

def set_dag_run_state_to_running(dag, execution_date, commit=False, session=None):
    set_dag_run_state(dag, execution_date, to_state=State.RUNNING, commit=commit, session=session)

```

The clock string is used by watchman to provide you the list of files that changed since that prior point in time.   The intended use is that you thread back the clock value result from one query and into the next so that you understand the incremental changes between those queries.   This matches the typical incremental `make` pattern used in most build tools.

It sounds like for your use case you just want to run the query and re-use the state if the files list is empty?

There are some features that help in computing and maintaining overall state.  I don't know if these quite match up to what you've described; these are better suited if you can perform incremental updates to the cached state.

## SCM aware queries

If you are using a mercurial repo you can use SCM aware queries.  These aren't in the main docs yet but the commit that introduced them has some reasonable info about using them:
https://github.com/facebook/watchman/commit/cb949e93018040c76458375b40538d8c4733e689

The intent is that you can use the mergebase to lookup your cached state, and then the list of changed files since that branch point in the repo to compute a delta.

We don't currently support git, but adding git support wouldn't be too much effort.  It is however not on our TODO list; we will happily accept PRs from folks that really need it sooner!

## Use the new `content.sha1hex` field to hash the files you care about

The docs for this also have not yet made it to the main site, but here's the commit that introduced them:
https://github.com/facebook/watchman/commit/c884bba3eb4ca7f03b4d9cf06774f3b50481c79d#diff-f7ca7066272dc8e646090e01d35434af

The idea here is that you'd set your query expression to match the files that are of interest in your cached state and then include the content hash in your query:

```
["query", "/some/path", {
   "expression":  ["match", "*.cpp"],
  "fields": ["name", "content.sha1hex"]
}]
```

This will cause watchman to give you a list of the matching files and their SHA1 content hashes.  You can then compute an overall hash for the state from this, as well as use the SHA1 content hash of individual files to determine whether you need to recompute state for that particular file.

@LouisCauchy 

You need: 
a textfile containing each wav complete transcript per line (utf8 encoded). Call this textfile, **vocabulary.txt**
You will also need to install Kenlm tools for the LM build : http://kheafield.com/code/kenlm/estimation/

Clean the original textfile:
- open the alphabet.txt textfile,
- feed in your own alphabet,
- save it
- open the original textfile,
- with your best editor, clean the file : all its characters MUST be present in alphabet.txt remove any punctuation, but you can keep the apostroph, if present in alphabet, as a character.

1 - Creating arpa file for binary build :
`/bin/bin/./lmplz --text vocabulary.txt --arpa  words.arpa --o 2`
-o param (“order model to estimate”)
It seems that for small corpus (my case of 400 wav files), a value from 2-4 seems to be the best

2 - creating binary file :
`/bin/bin/./build_binary -T -s words.arpa  lm.binary`

These were the directions that I followed to create my lm.binary. For more help look at [https://discourse.mozilla.org/t/tutorial-how-i-trained-a-specific-french-model-to-control-my-robot/22830](url)

@SylvainCorlay, my criticism of `_validate` Isn't that it accepts an instance, but rather that the tasks it performs (static-validation and cross-validation) should actually be done in separate methods. In other words the structure should be:

```python

def _validate(self, inst, value):
    value = _static_validate(value)
    if inst._cross_validation_lock is False:
        value = self._cross_validate(inst, value)
    return value
```

This way `_static_validate` can be invoked at the time the descriptor instance is created.

In this restructure:

1. `TraitType.validate(inst, value)` would actually be called during `_cross_validate` since that's what the signature of the method, which accepts the current instance, implies it should be doing.
2. From what I can tell, all current implementations of `TraitType.validate` in `traitlets` could actually be performed in `_static_validate`. Though doing this in a backward compatible way would require some thought.

I thought about this again today, and while you can reliably call `_<trait>_default` with traitlets 4.0, the fact that you can call this method doesn't mean it was ever actually used by the class it belongs to:

```python
class A(HasTraits):
	
	i = Int()
	
	def _i_default(self):
		return 1

class B(A):
	
	i = Int(2)

b = B()
true_default = b.i
false_default = b._i_default()

print(true_default)
print(false_default)
```

As it turns out, `_<trait>_default` is NOT a reliable way to discover a trait's default value.
So are we moving to emitter?

@SylvainCorlay I assume you're asking about the use-case I mentioned w/r/t validating example values?

In that case, the example isn't ever set on the instances itself: I just want to validate that it would pass TraitType checks **if it were set**.  The most straightforward way to do that, I think, is to call `TraitType._validate` while holding the cross validation lock.  Note that that's not equivalent to calling `TraitType.validate`, since `_validate` is what handles skipping validation of `None` if `allow_none` is True.

This .obj does indeed take forever. It is running, and so will eventually
finish, but not for a while. The problem (or at least the first) is in
objFileImporter.cpp:InternReadFile() It iterates through the buffer and
tries to remove backslashes followed by newlines. If you open you .obj in a
text editor and search for \, you'll find a bunch of them. They seem to
occur when you have exported a quad intstead of triangle.

So the code goes through the entire buffer checking for '\', removing it,
then removing all immediate consecutive '\r' and '\n' chars. Your .obj uses
\r\n for new lines. So we are doing 3 erases every time we find a '\'.

The std::vector::erase function has a complexity of O(n), ie. for a 102MB
file, very slow.

This hunt for backslashes takes approx 1/6th of the total import + post
process time (at least for my file), and I have no backslashes. So I've
been wandering about this. However never had much of a reason to bother
changing it.

The ideal solution would be to parse and skip these chars in the actually
parsing stage, therefore not having to do any erases.

Another option would be to use a STL container that had O(1) complexity for
removing items in the middle (ie. a list).

As a quick test I removed the \\\r\n in notepad++ and then the file import
took 4 minutes, and the entire thing about 5.5 minutes (my initial
estimate). Unfortunately it then failed with "bad allocation". So I'll look
into that as well.

@wimglenn that sort of thing is what I was thinking of. It's not ideal, but it does work if you're not sharing the same client across multiple threads. To make it slightly more tolerable you could scope the event handler like so:

```python
class SHA256Provider(object):
    """Provides a pre-computed content-sha256 for a botocore request."""
    def __init__(self, client, checksum, event_name=None, handler_id=None):
        self.checksum = checksum
        self._event_emitter = client.meta.events
        self._event_name = event_name or 'before-call.*.*'
        self._handler_id = handler_id or 'sha256-provider'

    def __enter__(self):
        self._event_emitter.register_first(
            self._event_name, self._set_content_sha256, self._handler_id
        )

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        self._event_emitter.unregister(
            self._event_name, self._set_content_sha256, self._handler_id
        )

    def _set_content_sha256(self, params, **kwargs):
        params['headers']['x-amz-content-sha256'] = self.checksum
```

You could then use this to make things slightly prettier:

```python
client = boto3.client('glacier')
checksum = 'precomputed'

with SHA256Provider(client, checksum):
    client.upload_multipart_part(
        vaultName='foo',
        uploadId='bar',
        range='1-2',
        body=b'baz'
    )
```

I had to do this recently. In the end the simplest solution that I found was simply to have a loop that alternately read chunks of data from the source `StreamingBody` and wrote to them the stdin pipe and then read as much as possible from the stdout pipe and wrote the result to my output stream (which was an SSL connection). The problem with `Popen.communicate()` is that as far as I can tell it attempts to send all of the input to the process before reading back (and buffering) all of the output. This will lock up if the subprocess writes in a blocking manner and the output is so large that it fills up the output pipe's buffers before you've fed it all the input and it will also fail if the output is too large to fit in memory even if you have succeeded in feeding in all of the input.. You need to drain the output and pass it on down the line before feeding it more input.

Global installation via `sudo pip` is really discouraged in the python world, yet we don't have any other way to install this globally. Using virtualenvs to create isolated environments is OK for python devs but is not a viable option for all end users. So I'd like to move on **creating a homebrew formula** sooner than later to at least get OS X users into the fray without much hassle.

Some decision points

1. Should we just [maintain our own tap](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/How-to-Create-and-Maintain-a-Tap.md) (really just a repo with a Ruby formula in it) or try to work with the Homebrew team to get mapbox [included in the homebrew repo](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/How-To-Open-a-Homebrew-Pull-Request-(and-get-it-merged).md)? The advantage to a Tap  is greater independence and lightweight  publishing and maintenance (just git push). The advantage to the main homebrew repo is easier discoverability, tighter quality control and slightly less typing to install (`brew install mapbox` versus `brew install mapbox/repo/mapbox`). For now, *I am leaning heavily towards using the Tap method* until we work out the kinks.

2.  If we go the Tap route, what should our repo name be? We already have a [mapbox/homebrew-custom](https://github.com/mapbox/homebrew-custom) repo (making the installation command `brew install mapbox/custom/mapbox`) but we might consider starting another `homebrew-____` repo for this and other "official" Mapbox apps.

Besides that, I've read through the [Python for Formula Authors](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew/blob/master/share/doc/homebrew/Python-for-Formula-Authors.md) docs and have a solid working formula ready to go once we decide on these details.

Historically it is possible to switch the driver within the context manager:
```
with browser:
    browser.request_library = LIB_REQUESTS
    browser.open()
```
As long as we maintain backwards compatibility regarding the `requests_library` attribute we do not know at the time we `__enter__` the context manager which driver will be used.
In fact it is even possible to switch / mix drivers within a test.

The request headers are therefore "cached" in the browser instance and applied to new driver instances uppon initialization. Since `login` is only an `Authorization` request header, this applies to `login` too.

The issue with the example above is that the `X-zope-handle-errors` header is [added by default by the driver](https://github.com/4teamwork/ftw.testbrowser/blob/850c857aba2dd4a23233f289c58284fd71652277/ftw/testbrowser/drivers/mechdriver.py#L126-L127) in order to have a good default behavior. This header does only make sense when using the mechdriver.

Todo:
If the `X-zope-handle-errors` is cleared before the mechbrowser is instantiated, the browser must ensure to clear the header after instantiating the driver at any time.

It's fortunate that so many of the internals of treq.testing are public in this way. The one caveat is that `treq.testing._SynchronousProducer` isn't, so you must either:

* Reimplement `_SynchronousProducer`; or
* Advance the clock enough for the cooperator inside [FileBodyProducer](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/api/twisted.web.client.FileBodyProducer.html) to send any request body, then [flush()](https://treq.readthedocs.io/en/release-17.8.0/api.html#treq.testing.RequestTraversalAgent.flush).

When doing this sort of no-I/O testing the [cooperator API](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/api/twisted.internet.task.Cooperator.html) seems to come up a lot. It seems to creep into places where it is difficult to override, like `FileBodyProducer` or [`twisted.internet._producer_helpers._PullToPush`](https://github.com/twm/yarrharr/pull/277/commits/1a5b5832edbf1fb1b1f45e9d99b65dad51ada566#diff-29ffe25b52ad7bddee9f2f08544e899cR98). Maybe it should be attached to the reactor directly, instead of coupled through the global module namespace?

-----

TBH I am not sure why `StubTreq` is a separate API, vs. documenting use of `HTTPClient` for testing.  I suppose `StubTreq` provides a few extra members — `text()`, `json()`, et. al., but those are also methods on an augmented response. It feels like we have a lot of duplicated API surface.

It is also _really_ weird to me that the `treq` module functions and particularly the `HTTPClient` functions accept an `agent` parameter. I feel that `HTTPClient` should encapsulate an agent (and implicitly the reactor the agent is associated with), rather than being a thing which... wraps up an agent you give it, except when you don't give it one it uses some default?

 -----

See also #226.
Thanks. It would be great if you could submit a PR later if httpbin fixes this issue too :)

The idea is the following: you start the flooding on the inverted image (as @jni suggests). When two lakes meet (at the lowest point between them, i.e. the "col") you compare the depth of the lakes. To the higher-valued minimum, you assign the difference between the current point ("col") and the value of the minimum. Then the lakes are fused. You continue doing this until every minimum but one got a value assigned. The last one gets the maximal value.

I've put some thought into creating a function that can select extrema by their distance to each other. I have written up a [quick demo of a naive algorithm that can handle the nd-case](http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/lagru/142a9613e2233cbde47dc783c071e717). 

However this has revealed 3 problems:

* I need to label each plateau. Sadly `skimage.measure.label` doesn't support more than 3 dimensions. Maybe I could do this with a consecutive flood fill that fills each plateau with a unique value. Seems like porting `label` to nd would be a good idea...
* Assuming that we want to use Cython I need a Python `set`-like container. I've done some googling but didn't find a satisfying solution. I think using Pythons `set` in Cython itself is our best bet. Although this prohibits us from releasing the GIL I think its the best solution compared to the other two I found / could think of:
  * Try to use `std::unordered_set` which is [included in Cython](https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/master/Cython/Includes/libcpp/unordered_set.pxd). This is a C++ feature and I'm not sure if this is wanted.
  * Maybe we could cook up our own solution for an unordered set but that would be far from optimized / perfect.
* This algorithm doesn't scale very well, the worst case executes the innermost loop N ** 2 with N being the number of maxima. This is worsened by the fact, that we are calling methods on a Python object when working with Python's set. Do you see a way to reduce the number of comparisons to make? Maybe we could iterate neighbors by their increasing distance to the current focus. That way we could break the inner loop early after a comparison fulfills the distance condition...

> What I do not understand is: why do you get the same results as extrema.local_maxima. Because you assume that a plateau that is higher than any pixel inside the image but touching the border is not a maximum. But in extrema.local_maxima this would be a maximum. So, in principle you should get some differences, no?

(Assuming you mean `morphology.local_maxima`) In the current implementation (b500723) touching the border is not a disqualifying criterion thus results is identical with `local_maxima`. According to the discussion around (A) I already have a modification in my local repo that prohibits maxima from touching the border. The performance is about the same.

> Another question I am asking myself: where does this enormous speed gain come from? Is this algorithmic or implementation?

I think the implementation in Cython plays definitely a role and `local_maxima` has a lot more Python around its core function `_greyreconstruction.reconstruction_loop`. Profiling would perhaps give more insight how much of that is due to the Python overhead and how much time is consumed.

@sciunto Even after your changes, I still feel like @emmanuelle that this is the only kind of deprecation. I would like to specify:

- a deprecation cycle is *not* necessary when
    * adding a new function, or
    * adding a new keyword argument to the *end* of a function signature, or
    * fixing what was buggy behaviour

- a deprecation cycle is necessary for *any breaking API change*, meaning a change where the function, invoked with the same arguments, would return a different result after the change. This includes:
    * changing the order of arguments or keyword arguments, or
    * adding arguments or keyword arguments to a function, or
    * changing a function's name or submodule, or
    * changing the default value of a function's arguments.

Are there others I'm forgetting?

Not sure what the best pattern is either. At least `preserve_range` is a start, though... without it, skimage is extraordinarily frustrating to use for my day-to-day tasks. Here is the case from today where I ran across the issue with pyramids: I want to correct an image from a microscope for uneven illumination, so I want to fit a particular image region to a low-order polynomial in 2D.

The images are big so the fitting is slow, so I want to use `pyramid_reduce()` to make a smaller version. **Problem 1:** now my original image and downsampled image are scaled differently. Easy enough fix, though, just need to remember the scale factor. Then I fit a polynomial to the intensities of the downsampled image to produce a "background image".

Now, to subtract the background image from the original image I just need to expand the background image back up with `pyramid_expand()`.

**Problem 2:** the background image exceeds 1 at a few points because it was generated from a polynomial fit. This causes `pyramid_expand()` to choke. Now, I need to rescale the image to [0,1] before enlarging it, and then undo both that rescaling and the first rescaling from `pyramid_reduce()`, just to get an image that I can apply to my microscope image. (The microscope image needs to stay in the units of original detector counts because I have a whole pipeline to convert detector counts into meaningful physical units).

One could even argue that there's no real reason that the transforms should bother enforcing dtype ranges in the first place. (Algorithms that actually need to make assumptions about pixel intensities -- sure, let them require the ranges be right. But transforms?) I'm glad  `preserve_range` is now available, but even so, lots of skimage remains broken-by-default for the vast majority of my tasks.

You have two main options:

1.
`fire.Fire()`
Then you can do `example.py function 1` or `example.py A function2 5` just like you suggested.
[You can also do `example.py A --param=5 function2 5` if you want to change the value of param in A's constructor.]

2.
```
fire.Fire({
  'function1': function1,
  'function2': A().function2,
})
```
Then you can do `example.py function1 4` or `example.py function2 5`, but you cannot change the value of param in A's constructor since you've hard-coded the call to the constructor.

----

There are various other ways to do this such as `A.function1 = function1; fire.Fire(A)` or `fire.Fire([function1, A])` or making a new class that exposes both an A and function1, but I think the above approaches (1+2) are better.

If we are to mimic the cumulative return for the algorithm that holding N equal weighted sub-portfolios and rebalancing them every N period, I wonder if it would make more sense to compute the one-period return as `np.nanmean((1 + ret) ** (1. / period) - 1)`? 

Compared to the original algorithm, it computes geometric mean before arithmetic mean. For a given period, the one-period return for the whole portfolio equals the average of one-period returns for N sub-portfolios. On the other hand, assuming each sub-portfolio grows at a steady rate within each rebalancing cycle, the one-period return for each sub-portfolio is given by the geometric mean of the corresponding N-period return, i.e. `(1 + ret)**(1. / period) - 1)`. Combining these two steps, gives the one-period return of the whole portfolio equals `np.nanmean((1 + ret) ** (1. / period) - 1)`.

Moreover, in the original algorithm, `((np.nanmean(ret) + 1) ** (1. / period)) - 1`, the N-period returns for each sub-portfolio is averaged first. Somehow that seems like all these N-period returns are realized at this particular period, while they are actually realized at subsequent N periods. So I think it would make more sense to first compute (1 + ret) ** (1. / period) - 1), as an estimation of the realized return for a sub-portfolio on the given period, and then to average over all the sub-portfolios.

I guess normally the main use of a hash is to let the object be a dictionary key.  `names[psf] = 'some name for this object'` or something.  (Seems a little backwards of good design in this case, but there's probably some real use case where this would be useful.)

So we could either do as you suggest and consider an Atmosphere unhashable if it's still in the undefined state.  Or we could just write the hash in the straightforward way and point out to the user that if they care about the hash for such purposes, they need to either do all hashing operations prior to drawing or all after drawing, since the drawing step will change the hash.  

I was going to suggest that the hash just be the same for all three cases (i.e. don't include the internal state in the hash), but I don't think that actually solves anything in the case of it being used as a dict key, since it has to go back to an equality test anyway, and I do think we want these to test as not equal.

It is just my experience that it leads to problems if you import directly. Maybe not in your case *now*, but if  `__init__.py` imports another package "ABC", and you install ``typed_ast`` a new virtualenv, where "ABC" is not installed yet, then "ABC" needs to be installed by hand before ``setup.py`` can run, because ``setup.py`` cannot run because it is dependent on that package via ``__init__.py``.  And if the environment in which you create your package has "ABC", you'll never noticed that until you install the package in a clean environment (e.g. if you run ``tox`` for testing)

Now you might be sure such a dependency will never happen. and that you will notice, but why run the risk? It is not as if the performance of `setup.py` is influenced that much now by doing the parsing.

All of this points to implementation in a management utility, rather than in the Patroni daemon code.  It also duplicates some functionality available from container orchestation systems, although those systems don't have any PostgreSQL knowledge.

Couple more things I'd like to see a second opinion on.

1. Having the command line interpret arguments as YAML values is a potential footgun. `edit-command --pg archive_command=true` will actually set it to `True`. This can be solved by encapsulating the value with quotes, but it could be surprising. I would just store the string, but for values outside `postgresql.parameters` we actually need to get the type correct, otherwise Patroni will start giving nasty exceptions. I think the whole thing would be best solved by making the config system know the types of the datastructure and do some validation. But I think that's something for a separate patch. Maybe just make a special case for postgresql.parameters and handle the rest later?
2. Any opinions for the naming of the commands? Current names are only placeholders. One idea  would be to merge the two commands and have the default just display the config and a `--edit` option to invoke the editor. And I don't have a good idea how to name it in a way that it's clear that `patronictl configuration` is for the patronictl command, but the new thing is for a Patroni config stored in the DCS.

With 2 nodes it's not possible to distinguish between master going down and a temporary network issue.

The scenario is this - the network glitches out for a little while, standby thinks the master is down, becomes the master. The network comes back and now you have 2 masters. If you are lucky your application is only using one of them, if not, you now have 2 different databases containing your data and no sane way to merge them.

If you try to fix this scenario properly you will end up with having to either have a voting quorum, i.e. 3 or more nodes, or an outside source to arbitrate master selection. Either way, 2 nodes will not work.

Those are not PostgreSQL, but Patroni logs, so changing PostgreSQL parameters won't affect them. Why do you think they are 'unnecessary'? Patroni reports every iteration of the event loop its role and who is the leader. Knowing that is important in order to understand, for instance, why did the failover happen.

You can mute the INFO messages from the event loop by setting the level in a `ha.py`.

We may also provide some way of controlling log levels in the future. Feel free to contribute a PR if you are interested.

## Breaking changes for simulation initialisation:

### Entity id and roles
Each `entity` manages the arrays containing the entity id and role for each individual: (former `idfam`, `quifam`...), through 4 attributes:
```py
entity.members_entity_id # Which entity does each individual belongs to
entity.members_role # Which role has each individual in this entity (role is an object)
entity.members_legacy_role # Which role has each individual in this entity (role is a number)
entity.members_position # Which position has each individual in the entity. Mainly used for internal business.
  ```
See an example [here](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/blob/4.0.0a1/openfisca_core/tests/test_entities.py#L35)
Attributes `entity.index_for_person_variable_name` and `entity.role_for_person_variable_name` therefore don't exist anymore.

### Simulation entities
`simulation.entity_by_key_singular` has been renamed to `simulation.entities`.
`simulation.entity_by_key_plural` has been deprecated.

### Other changes
`entity.is_persons_entity` has been renamed to `entity.is_person`

Codez la loi ! OpenFisca permet la transcription du code législatif en code informatique. Son moteur de calcul aide à comprendre les lois, analyser leurs changements et simuler des situations. Entièrement libre et gratuit, modulable et réutilisable, OpenFisca transforme le droit en logiciel.

DECOUVREZ COMMENT

OpenFisca est un moteur ouvert de microsimulation du système socio-fiscal. Un de ses objectifs, pédagogique, est de permettre d’aider les citoyens à mieux comprendre les réformes qui impactent leur quotidienne. OpenFisca et OpenFisca France sont des projets libres. Cela signifie que vous pouvez utiliser les logiciels associés, les installer, étudier leur code source, les modifier, les redistribuer de la façon que vous souhaitez. Une seule condition est imposée : les travaux dérivés d’OpenFisca doivent eux aussi être libres. There is a long discussion about this. We realize we need to figure out a way of generating samples (or maxes, or ...) quickly without copying values from the GPU to the CPU or between different parts of CPU memory. Unfortunately, due to DyNet's design simplicity (everything inside DyNet is a differentiable function), things that return non-differentiable values (e.g., discrete indices or symbols) don't really have a good place to live. We  need a general way of adding non-differentiable forward-triggering operations (e.g., forward(), scalar_value(), npvalue(), ...) so requests like this can be handled, but we haven't yet designed this.

Why just BiRNNs? Shouldn't any RNN allow efficiently getting just the final state?
In Keras, for example, this is the default, and you need to set `return_sequences=True` to get all the outputs from the intermediate time points too.
Another option is to keep `transduce` for both, but add a `final_state` boolean argument, with default value `False` (to keep backward compatibility). This naming is analogous to the `initial_state()` function.

    destination = f'postgres/{table_name}/{org_id}.csv'

        target=lambda b, r: blob.upload_from_file(r),
        name='upload-blob:%s' % destination,
        args=(blob, file))

    query = f'SELECT * FROM {table_name} WHERE organisation_id={org_id} LIMIT 10000'

    # writes into memory, but moves cursor all the time

@jaromrax: I'm closing this... because what you are seeing is expected behavior, as I understand it.  Basically, you are spawning different processes, so you shouldn't really see the `print` from these different processes unless they are all piped to the same `stdout`.  This is what I'd expect, and that is why the `ThreadPool` behavior is different.  If you do want to see the prints from each, you'll have to either return what you want printed, or somehow hook up the `stdout` from each process to the originating process.  Please reopen if I'm misinterpreting what you are asking.

So - the Task subclass I am referencing still has an invalidated block. But it had 'cache_target_dirs` to false.

Before the earlier change, I manually called create_results_dir so that I could
a) continue to use a familiar output directory 
b) toggle caching (with cache_target_dirs) to True and it would "just work"

But with current master, the create_results_dir call bails out if the target is valid, but that causes an Exception to raise when the task later references `vt.results_dir`.

If you look at the tests I added, they essentially do what I say above - they use invalidation blocks but are manually managing their results_dir creation. Presumably a real task would do what I do in real life and later set the results of the create_results_dir [to the cache] (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/task/task.py#L512)

I don't need this change - there is obvious ways around the problem and I already took one. But it seemed to me like calling create_results_dir should actually result in those directories always existing. Otherwise it should be privatized somehow.

I basically agree with your last statement - there is some bleed between the cache_manager and task that could be cleaned up. I am a couple nitpicks away on [a refactor](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/4168/files) that may get us closer.

I have seen that you now only take out the keys which you are able to parse:
https://github.com/genomoncology/related/blob/master/src/related/functions.py#L99-L118

```python
def to_model(cls, value):
    """
    Coerce a value into a model object based on a class-type (cls).
    :param cls: class type to coerce into
    :param value: value to be coerced
    :return: original value or coerced value (value')
    """

    if isinstance(value, cls) or value is None:
        pass  # skip if right type or value is None

    elif issubclass(cls, Enum):
        value = cls(value)

    elif is_model(cls) and isinstance(value, dict):
        value = convert_key_to_attr_names(cls, value)
        value = cls(**value)

    else:
        value = cls(value)

    return value


def convert_key_to_attr_names(cls, original):
    """ convert key names to their corresponding attribute names """
    attrs = fields(cls)
    updated = {}

    for a in attrs:
        key_name = a.metadata.get('key') or a.name
        if key_name in original:
            updated[a.name] = original.get(key_name)

    return updated
```

Would it make sense to have an optional flag in `to_model`, say `no_extra_keys` that would raise an error in case the user specified some extra fields?

Hi,

There is a Device Type identifier (DD) command in the AT commands (Addressing commands), page 128 of the document:
>DD
>Device Type Identifier. Stores a device type value. This value can be used to
>differentiate different XBee-based devices. Digi reserves the range 0 - 0xFFFFFF.
>For example, Digi currently uses the following DD values to identify various ZigBee
>products:
>0x30001 - ConnectPort X8 Gateway
>0x30002 - ConnectPort X4 Gateway
>0x30003 - ConnectPort X2 Gateway
>...
>CRE 
>0 - 0xFFFFFFFF 
>0x30000

I think in the case of the ND command they added the DD (Device Type Identifier) to the answer payload (for some reason they might have started to have the need for that info), and because of that when the answer is parsed you get the, _ValueError_.
