I was afraid of this! Beneath `PandasHDFStore` are pandas, pytables, and HDF5, and all have limitations. I have a few suggestions, and others can weigh in:

1. Try `PandasHDFStoreSingleNode`, which literally stores all of your data in a single on-disk table. This may actually scale *better* than storing frames separately, since HDF5 is optimized for tens of nodes in a file, not 10^5 (`trackpy.PandasHDFStoreBig` is a partial workaround for this limitation). This is a relatively thin wrapper around `pandas.HDFStore` in "table" mode, which offers database-like indexing and querying features. This would in principle help with doing particle-wise queries. A thorough reading of the relevant Pandas (and maybe pytables) documentation could allow you to get decent performance.
- Go one level lower and use pytables directly. There is some slippage between pytables and pandas that may be leading to problems. Again, this should make particle-wise queries possible.
- Partition your data set into tens of "shard" files, each with a more manageable number of frames. Write a `framewise_data` class that comprises many `PandasHDFStoreBig` objects.
- Roll up your sleeves and use `sqlite`, which is an industrial-strength relational database. I suspect that would be a pretty steep learning curve unless you have prior SQL experience.

…and one more totally wild idea: If you have few dropped/added particles over the course of your movie, that means you have a reasonable upper bound *M* on the particle ID. You could then use pytables or h5py to store your data in a MxNxc array, where *c* is the number of data columns. Even though many entries would be blank, with appropriate compression this would not use a ton of extra space. `pytables` and especially `h5py` have strong support for this; you would probably want to use the "chunked" layout for your HDF5 file so that framewise writing and reading would have tolerable performance. This is arguably the simplest solution of all, though it is the most radical departure from the others.

After some discussion with @bacongobbler, instead of _only_ letting operators opt-in to a control / worker partitioning as in this PR, we can instead allow operators to opt into (or out of) more finely-grained metadata that states what components each machine is eligible to run.

@bacongobbler, what do you think that metadata looks like?  I am thinking something along the lines of `<component name>Eligible=true`.

Example:

```
  fleet:
    public-ip: $private_ipv4
    etcd_request_timeout: 3.0
    metadata: controllerEligible=true builderEligible=true appEligible=true ...
```

Also, do you think it's worthwhile to also maintain the notion of a "general purpose" role?  This way the included `user-data.example` could simply look like the following and not have to change every time a new component is introduced:

```
  fleet:
    public-ip: $private_ipv4
    etcd_request_timeout: 3.0
    metadata: deisRole=general
```

Units, could thus be as follows (using controller for example):

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata="controllerEligible=true"
MachineMetadata="deisRole=general"
```

Also, if these values need to be configurable and not *true* constants, I would propose making them configurable using environment variables.

```python
LOOKAHEAD_PERIODS = int(os.environ.get('PYEVM_SHARING_LOOKAHEAD_PERIODS', 4))
```

This pattern is fine, however, it will execute once at module load and be done.  I'd suggest something more akin to this so that it can be changed at runtime.

```python
def get_sharding_config():
    return {
        'LOOKAHEAD_PERIODS': int(os.environ.get('PYEVM_SHARING_LOOKAHEAD_PERIODS', 4)),
        ...
    {
```

This way anything that needs these constants, or to modify them at runtime can do so.

> Other ideas are to move the PAYGAS state change to occur outside of whatever snapshot/revert block it's currently in, moving it to a location after the reversion happens when a computation errors out.

I've thought about this before but it seems there could be a DoS attack vector. If `PAYGAS` state change happens after transaction execution(i.e., in the post computation part), chances are that who is responsible for paying the transaction fee might turn out to be unable to afford it(transferred his ether away in the process) and hence render the transaction ineffective.

How about we don't make the state change immediately but instead store the transaction fee information in computation/msg object(or along with PAYGAS gas price)? Like a debt that liquidates only at the end of the transaction?
During transaction execution, whenever there's a value transfer take place we first check if this is paid by the account who's responsible for the transaction fee and if this value transfer will make him become insolvent. If yes, throw `InsufficientFund` exception and charge him the transaction fee.
This issue will be resolved in https://github.com/ethereum/populus/pull/474

@kalefranz , yes this PR gives me what I want. I took the extra directory out in the latest commit to run some tests on my side, but I could add it back in.

Also, after giving it some thought, I have changed my stance somewhat.

I think the root_dir should be in the envs dir, but if its not writable, it should not be in the pkgs dir. Say I'm an admin, and I have a global anaconda install with a few envs set up I think my users will find useful. I want them to be able to use those, and I want them to be able to create their own. Lets say a user tries to install a package to an env in a non writable env_dir. It should pop up with that 'this is how you clone an environment message', and fail gracefully.

As long as we're talking about what I want ( ;-) ), I don't think its necessarily a bad idea for the pkgs dir to include a non writable directory. It would be awesome if conda had a feature where it copy the package from the root env dir to a user pkg_dir where it can extract it or do whatever it is that does that requires write access. We have some fairly large environments that take quite a bit of download time.

OK, this silent swallowing of hard-link errors just cost our developer group more time this morning, spent troubleshooting new environments that `conda create` reported nothing wrong with.  But audits of these environments identified some missing packages files (all of which are read-only by group in the package cache).

Long-term, issue #2437 is going to require some fix (like `chmod g+w` after extracting a package) which will need testing.  But short-term, we need to be notified when `conda [create|install]` encounters an error.  All this pull-request does is change one call from a `log.error` to `sys.exit`.  This is definitely a temporary fix.  There are already 3 `sys.exit` calls in that function.  Yes, long term, those calls should be refactored away.  Short term, conda needs to report these link errors.

I'm not opposed to that in principle, but we'd need to make sure it's backwards-compatible.  For better or for worse, if an existing config file has a line like

```
foo : bar = baz
```

then that will parse to an entry where ``'foo : bar'`` maps to a value of ``baz``.

However, if there was an option you could pass to the ``configobj.ConfigObj`` class to indicate that you wanted to use colons as a separator, that would probably be okay.

(Theoretically we could use colon as a separator if and only if there are no equals signs on a line.  However, I'm dubious about introducing heuristics and guesswork to the parser.)

Is there any way to create many instances of the VMs from the copy of the snapshot 
Short answer, No. The VHD is for the single specific machine. You can clone the VHD and create a new VM from it but likely it will conflict with the first one, say, the same cached private IP address, dns name, etc. Those are pretty hard to tweak. 
If you like to reuse the same customization, the recommended flow is you generalize the VHD to be an image and create new VMs from the image.
If you like to create multiple VMs with almost the same configuration, try scale-set w/o load balancer.

The command $ cat $(which az) @derekbekoe pointed out solved the issue.  In my case I edit the az file as simply changing the last line python to python3:

`#!/bin/bash

SOURCE="${BASH_SOURCE[0]}"
while [ -h "$SOURCE" ]; do # resolve $SOURCE until the file is no longer a symlink
  DIR="$( cd -P "$( dirname "$SOURCE" )" && pwd )"
  SOURCE="$(readlink "$SOURCE")"
  [[ $SOURCE != /* ]] && SOURCE="$DIR/$SOURCE" # if $SOURCE was a relative symlink, we need to resolve it relative to the path where the symlink file was located
done
DIR="$( cd -P "$( dirname "$SOURCE" )" && pwd )"

export PYTHONPATH="${DIR}/src:${PYTHONPATH}"

python3 -m azure.cli "$@"`

python3 above will automatically point to /usr/bin/python3 and solved the issue.

Resource references are currently inconvenient to follow, because there's no get() that allow you to fetch using a reference id. So you need to parse the names of the resources that make up the id.

In this case, you need to parse the resource id to get the names 'myresourcegroup', 'app-green04-nic' and 'ipconfig1'.  With these, you'll be able to access the NIC with `network_client.network_interfaces.get('myresourcegroup, 'app-green04-nic')`.

Looking at LoadBalancerOperations, it looks like you should be able to do a get(), modify the contents of the load_balancer.backend_address_pools[0].backend_ip_configurations by adding/modifying/removing ResourceId objects, and then call create_or_update() with the modified LoadBalancer object.

Obtaining a resource id to create a ResourceId is easy, no need to build the string yourself, you can usually just call a get() method and Azure will populate and return the id for you.  For example, to get the resource id for a NIC configuration:

```
result = network_client.network_interfaces.get('myresourcegroup', 'app-green04-nic')
result.network_interface.ip_configurations[0].name
#ipconfig1
result.network_interface.ip_configurations[0].id
#/subscriptions/xxx-xxx-xxx/resourceGroups/myresourcegroup/providers/Microsoft.Network/networkInterfaces/app-green04-nic/ipConfigurations/ipconfig1
```

You can't index by name on those collections, so you'll need to iterate to find the one you are looking for.

In Bullet, joints/links and their limits/motors are split between btMultiBody, btMultiBodyLink and btMultiBodyConstraint (in case you use multibody. to use yaml *env* configs you can do so

```python 
def construct_yaml_env(self, node):
    value = [x.strip() for x in self.construct_scalar(node).split(',')]
    if not (3 > len(value) > 0) or not value[0]:
        raise yaml.YAMLError(
            u'!!env {}, not trafaret '
            u'!!env ENV_VARIABLE, DEFAULT_VAL_OPTIONAL'.format(
                self.construct_scalar(node)))

    if len(value) == 1:
        value = os.environ[value[0]]

    value = os.environ.get(*value)

    return yaml.load(value)

yaml.add_constructor(u'tag:yaml.org,2002:env', construct_yaml_env)

```

The notebook no longer relies on IPython, which provides one of many kernels. For convenience the notebook does, however, add the IPython kernel for the notebook server's env *if no Python kernel is already registered*. That IF is the source of frustration, if you want to be doing it all the time, though. The idea being that changing the env of the notebook server *should not* change the env of your kernels. Similarly, making an env with a kernel doesn't require installing the notebook in that env, just the kernel. This is my habit - I frequently make envs and install IPython in them, and register a kernelspec for the env. What I don't do is install the notebook server multiple times.

We could also make the in-env kernel available unconditionally with a *different* name, though. It's more special treatment of IPython than we wanted, but it may be appropriate to put this behavior back.

There's another hack you can do, and I'm not sure if/how we can make it more convenient to do so: Change the absolute path for Python in the kernel.json to just `python` instead of `/path/to/bin/python3`. If you do this, the kernel will always use the first `python` on PATH in the notebook server's process, which is generally the env's Python.

The idea for using it with nbgrader is that it should be quite difficult/annoying to delete cells that are marked as undeleteable, because removing them will totally break nbgrader.

I would guess there are kind of two competing use cases for this functionality. The first, like how nbgrader uses it, is a way for applications that produce notebooks to control how users use those notebooks. The second is a way for users to "lock" cells to prevent themselves from accidentally deleting them. In the first case, there shouldn't be an easy way to toggle it except programmatically; in the second case, there should be. So I guess it depends on what type of functionality the undeletable feature is meant to be "for".

> the existence of a schema does not mean you must have a value, even a null, for every field. 

But isn't the semantic of null/`None`, in the context of a `IntType` field, just "there is no value here"? The only time we have to distinguish "there is no value here" and "there is no value here and ignore this when using me as a patch" is when dealing with a "partial" model.

If we don't want to rethink how partial works, then your proposal here is the way to go. But if changing how partial is done in on the table for the 2.0 release, then I believe we can get something nicer. For illustration purpose:

```python
class M(Model):
    a, b, c = IntType(), IntType(default=2), IntType()

print type(M)  # => schematics.models.ModelMeta
print type(M.Patch)  # => schematics.models.ModelPatchMeta
m = M({'a': 1})
print m.a, m.b, m.c  # => 1, 2, None
print m._data  # => {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c' None}. Every slot is filled with a regular model instance.
m.b = None
print m.b  # => None, because default is only applied on instantiation.

patch = M.Patch({'a': ''})
 # => ConversionError. Patch facilitates data importing and enforcing schema.
patch = M.Patch({'a': 3})
# => equivalent to the backward-compatible form: M({'a': 3}, partial=True)
print p._data  # => {'a': 1}. Patch only contains explicitly provided data.
print p.a  # => 1
print p.b  # => raise AttributeError. default value (2) is never applied to a patch
print p.c  # => raise AttributeError
p.validate()  # => TypeError: Patch does not support validation.
m.patch(patch)  # => Apply patch, recursively if patch contains nested patches
print m.a, m.b, m.c  # => 3, 2, None
```

By separating Patch into its own concept, both Patch and regular Model become easier to reason about. Patch cannot run validate, does not work with PolyModelType,  can only contain patches in its (Poly)ModelType fields, etc etc; Model always have all its attributes filled (possibly with `None`), can be patched, cannot contain patches in its (Poly)ModelType fields, etc. By assuming these differences, both are now more predictable and they won't intertwine.

As a side note, what do @kracekumar, @bintoro, and @jfitzpatrick99 think of the docs and test patches, particularly the semantics? In a nutshell, calling `MyModel(strict=<value>)`:

- Behaves exactly as normal if `None` (the default) is given
- Forces strict importing if `True`
- Forces unstrict importing if `False`

I might be wrong, but I think this is a misunderstanding of what the `convert` method does. It exists to convert an unvalidated dict into the right types. For example...

```python
In [1]: from schematics.models import Model

In [2]: from schematics.types import IntType

In [3]: class Foo(Model):
   ...:     bar = IntType()
   ...:

In [5]: foo = Foo()

In [6]: foo.convert({"bar": "10"})
Out[6]: {'bar': 10}
```

I think the issue here is that you're trying to use `m.convert` to add the values of the dict to `m`.  You can do this by just passing it into the constructor.
```python
In [3]: class MyModel(Model):
   ...:     provider = StringType(required=True, choices=['a','b','c'])
   ...:

In [4]: m = MyModel({'provider': 'a'})

In [5]: m.to_dict()
Out[5]: {'provider': u'a'}
```

This will, at the very least, catch things like rogue fields in the dict you pass to the constructor. If you wanted to run the actual field validators (e.g. `choices`), you have to explicitly call the `validate` on `m` before `to_dict` to raise the exception.

```python
In [4]: m = MyModel({'provider': 'a'})

In [5]: m.validate()

In [6]: m.to_dict()
Out[6]: {'provider': u'a'}
```

```python
print grad(lambda x: x*x)(2.5)                     # prints 5.

If so, you can get that behavior by using `getval` from autograd.util:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd.util import getval

consider_constant = getval

print grad(lambda x: x*x)(2.5)                     # prints 5.
print grad(lambda x: x*consider_constant(x))(2.5)  # prints 2.5
```

If that's useful, we should probably add a `consider_constant` function (even if it's just another name for `getval`). One wrinkle is that `getval` doesn't recursively descend into data structures that might contain Nodes, so we might want to write something a bit more general.

Regarding `rpc_get_all_names()`, one option might be to calculate a name's "id" as `int(sha256(name,register_block_height)[0:16])` (i.e. the upper 64 bits of the SHA256 of the name and the block at which it was registered).  This would get us a deterministic and roughly even distribution of name IDs over the range `[0, 2**63)`.  Then, to select page `p`, you would:

* find the number of unexpired names (`SELECT COUNT(name) FROM ... WHERE (unexpired)`).  If zero, then return.
* calculate id range `R` as `2**63 / num_unexpired_names`
* make sure the signed values `p * R` and `(p + 1) * R` fit into 63 bits
* run `SELECT name FROM ... WHERE id >= p * R AND id < (p + 1) * R`

The underlying C library, libpostal, is not thread-safe, so releasing the GIL and allowing threads will almost certainly have unintended consequences.

Libpostal is quite fast - as in process every address in OpenStreetMap in just over an hour on a single core fast. IMHO it's premature optimization to worry about multithreading unless there's a concrete use case where greater throughput is needed _within_ a single process (it's totally fine to use multiple shared-nothing processes, MapReduce, etc. on multicore machines).

@johnsca, I think that this is a very interesting approach.  The name change from 'state' -> 'flag' is very sensible as 'state' is such a loaded word in the context of software systems!

Unlike @galgalesh I don't really have an objection to:

```python
admin = flag('config.set.admin')
```

It doesn't look like a constructor to me; rather it's just a function call on `flag` with a single string parameter.  On a literal reading of it, it looks like it returns a `flag` thing which corresponds to `config.set.admin`.

I am concerned about backwards/forwards compatibility and hope that we can find someway of supporting the current mechanism/template for writing interfaces and their usage.  Otherwise we're forcing a change on currently working interfaces.

I consider #1882 and #2071 basically the same feature request, to modify the list of domains whether one is to add domains and the other is to remove.

My intention with this issue was tangential to those, but as I [wrote in a comment to #2071](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/issues/2071#issuecomment-171041891) I begin to suspect that any change in the list of domains to an existing folder would require one to specify which folder to modify.
The suggestion here would be one way to solve that.

My main argument in this issue is still the request to know where the certs will be stored before running the client. Thus avoiding having to parse the output of the client to determine where it placed the certs.

Hi @pganssle,
I find the current API somewhat confusing.

1. `tz.gettz()` should be the main way people get timezones, because it tries the system timezones first.
2. `zoneinfo.gettz()` creates a `ZoneInfoFile` instance from the embedded `zoneinfo` file on the first time it is called, and gets the timezones from it. I would prefer it if users simply instantiated the class themselves if they really need it.

So the plan was to:

1. Make sure our main users don't use `zoneinfo.gettz()`. (As you can see in this thread, pandas already switched)
2. Add a deprecation warning for one version. (Say 2.6.0)
3. Remove the function on the next version. (Say 2.7.0)

I haven't got around to do any of the above, and I am not sure if the plan is still relevant. What's your opinion on this?

@wagner-certat What would be the advantage of doing this in the parser? I'm pretty sure that this would be needlessly complicated to add into an "automagical" date parser when you have to know it's a date in the first place to even consider parsing it, and you *must* know that it's already an LDAP date, at which point it's easy enough to do this:

```python
from datetime import datetime

EPOCH_TO_LDAP = (datetime(1970, 1, 1) - datetime(1601, 1, 1)).total_seconds()

def ldap_parser(dt_str):
    dt_int = int(dt_str)

    # Convert to seconds
    int_seconds = (dt_int) / 1000000000

    # Shift this until it's relative to 1971
    int_seconds -= EPOCH_TO_LDAP

    # Convert from an epoch time
    dt = datetime.fromtimestamp(int_seconds)

    return dt

mydate = '13101530460000000000'

assert ldap_parser(mydate) == datetime(2016, 3, 3, 21, 1)
```

I suppose if it's widely used we could put some sort of one-off special function in `parser` that handles it.

`telethon.tl.types`. [Quote](https://lonamiwebs.github.io/Telethon/):
> Methods, also known as *requests*, are used to interact with the Telegram API itself and are invoked with a call to `.invoke()`. **Only these** can be passed to `.invoke()`! You cannot `.invoke()` types or constructors, only requests. […]

I've asked several people about what do they expect when they try to invoke a "type" but I'm yet to have a clear answer, sigh.

Regarding your problem, you can't just make up a new type like that. [The scheme is just the way it is](https://github.com/telegramdesktop/tdesktop/blob/dev/Telegram/Resources/scheme.tl), and it's what's running in the server. If you made up a new type, the server would not be able to recognize it, and fail in a different way.

> The [Telegram API documents  a way](https://core.telegram.org/type/InputUser) […]

You are aware that the latest layer available there is 23, and Telethon is running layer 66! The official documentation is highly outdated, on respect to the available requests and types.

The loom viewer has such a mechanism, by calling the tiling/expansion commands with one of two different flags: `--clean` (or `-C`) to remove the selected caches, and `--truncate` (or `-t`) to overwrite existing caches, so there always is a fall-back.

Actually, do we need timestamps? The main need for me was keeping track of *modifications*; something as simple as an integer counter that increases by one every time a file gets modified is sufficient there too (if we use a 64-bit integer and record a 1000 modifications per second, it would still take roughly 600 million years to overflow). 

Keeping track of *when* it was modified is a different problem, although I suppose there could be some use for that too: if a data-set becomes corrupted or is tampered with, time-stamps could be useful to track down when that might have happened (although there is nothing stopping anyone from tampering with the time-stamp either).

If we do go with time-stamps, I propose that to use the [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) standard of formatting (so something like `20180116T184331Z`), to ensure consistency between libraries. It also happens to make modification checks as simple as a greater-than string comparison.

This PR was blocked on figuring out what to do with the test failures from negative timeouts. It turns out that with the binary protocol, negative timeouts are actually invalid, which I've had them clarify in the specification:

Ideally libmemcached or pylibmc would raise a `ValueError` when using a negative timeout with the binary protocol, but they don't at present (see https://github.com/lericson/pylibmc/issues/202#issuecomment-187605421 onwards).

In the meantime I think django-pylibmc should just skip the negative timeout tests when using the binary protocol, and document that it's not a valid value.

`FuncDecl` could not use `translate` from `AST`, cause `AST.translate()` is creating an `AST` java object, which cannot be cast to its heir class object `FuncDecl`. Doing so will yield a runtime exception.

In order to fix this, we need to implement either a `FuncDecl.translate()` like I did in this branch, or a dispatcher in `AST` to dispatch its object creation in `AST.translate()` to heir class constructors. (like `Expr.create()` in `Expr.translate()`, but maybe this could be done easier with java reflection)

> I don't know if a note in the docs suffices though... this sounds like something that could bite people without them even realising it

Yes, I think merely documenting it is not the solution.

> What is a use case where a cycle of `Layer` instances would come up though?

None so far. You need to tinker with `Layer.input_layer` directly to construct a cycle. It just occurred to me that introducing a `__del__` method has some theoretical disadvantage.

> We could instead have `Layer.params = []` and `Layer.param_tags = {}`, using the list to maintain the ordering.
> Oh, I see @f0k already mentioned that method earlier.

Not directly, I was thinking about an own `OrderedDict` class that uses a `list` instead of the doubly linked list. (The `weakref` implementation of Python 3.4 is arguably more efficient.)

> It would be a pretty minimal change, and might even be clearer than currently (I wouldn't expect `Layer.params` to have anything to do with tags without reading the code).

Yes, it would be cleaner, but it would also make some operations more cumbersome. Disabling the bias in a layer after constructing it can currently be achieved by:
```python
del layer.params[layer.b]
layer.b = None
```
It would become:
```python
layer.params.remove(layer.b)
del layer.param_tags[layer.b]
layer.b = None
```
Collecting all the parameters of another layer to make them your own can currently be achieved by:
```python
self.params.update(other.params)
```
It would become:
```python
self.params.extend(other.params)
self.param_tags.update(other.param_tags)
```
So you'd always need to ensure the two stay in sync. I'm not really opposed to it, though. `Layer.get_params()` wouldn't be a lot different from what it's now. Having `Layer.params` be a `list` would probably be less surprising than the `OrderedDict`, but it may lead to `Layer.param_tags` being overseen.

@nicain thanks for exploring this issue. I'll have a look at your branch. Currently my feeling is that a) having Python 2 support is a significant benefit but also b) maintaining Python 2 and 3 simultaneously requires significant effort. Essentially it seems that we have two main options:

1) Maintain Python 2/3 compliant codes
----------------------------------------
+ No separate versions and Python 2/3 are always in sync
- Python 2 developers introducing errors for Python 3 and vice/versa
- All developers have to know and maintain Python 2/3 compliance 
- Python 2 is scheduled to die in 2020 
- There is still a lot to do until the SfN release and making this switch right now would likely slow development

2) Have a separate fork for Python 2
-------------------------------------
+ Most developers can work in Python 3 only
+ Clean code base, i.e, clear separation of Python 2 and 3
+ Allows us to do Python 2 only for main releases
+/- One developers has to deal with porting and maintaining Python 2 
+/- Tests for Python 2 done independent of tests for Python 3
- Python 2 and 3 repos can become out-of-sync

I would suggest that we schedule a phone call at some point during the next weeks to discuss this issue to define how we want to deal with this issue. Right now my inclination is that at least until SfN we might want to do this via separate forks to reduce the risk of not being able to complete the features we want for the release. Once we have completed the release, at which point most major changes should hopefully be done, we can then review what we are doing for Python2/3 to decide how we want to move forward in the long run. 

These are just my current thoughts. I still need to review the Python 2 port. I am open for discussion, but I think it would be helpful to do this in person via videocon at some point.

> I really think there are better patterns to insert something

Erwin suggested you use a loop instead. I don't really yet know what that means for us. 

Either way, we could perhaps instead reject on a Python level if `inserted = result.fetchone()` becomes `None`. If that happens, we know you called `create()` with the same ID (and parent_id and collection_id) but with different `last_modified`). It could push the problem onto the calling client. 

The reason that's probably a good idea is that one of the clients did get an OK so it would not make sense to update the record for that other client. 

Meaning, if it's true that two concurrent clients attempt the same `.create()` (with possibly different JSON!) it would be weird to "correct" the second client's call.

Unfortunately we just saw the offending error a few more times, from the `_get_schema_column_info` query. I looked at the explain output for that query, and noticed this:

```
->  LD Seq Scan on pg_class c  (cost=0.00..23.10 rows=163 width=136)
      Filter: pg_table_is_visible(oid)
```

The query isn't calling that function directly, so I believe the source of this function call is the use of the `pg_table_def` view, which is defined like:

```
 SELECT n.nspname AS schemaname, c.relname AS tablename, a.attname AS "column", format_type(a.atttypid, a.atttypmod) AS "type", format_encoding(a.attencodingtype::integer) AS "encoding", a.attisdistkey AS "distkey", a.attsortkeyord AS "sortkey", a.attnotnull AS "notnull"
   FROM pg_namespace n, pg_class c, pg_attribute a
  WHERE n.oid = c.relnamespace AND c.oid = a.attrelid AND a.attnum > 0 AND NOT a.attisdropped AND pg_table_is_visible(c.oid)
  ORDER BY n.nspname, c.relname, a.attnum;
```

I'm not sure I see why this view is being used. You're already joining with `pg_attribute` as well, and the only thing you take from `pg_table_def` is `column`, which you could equally well get from `pg_attribute.attname`. Is it for the `a.attnum > 0 AND NOT a.attisdropped` stuff? I'd be a shame to have to duplicate that in the query, but that might be what's necessary to fix this for us. On the plus side, removing use of `pg_table_def` would remove the need to monkey with the search path.

No difference. JSON structure is based on **several** internal Intel tools developer prior to this SDK. One of these tools expects `aux-param-colorcorrection` to be the name of the JSON field while the other calls the same thing `param-colorcorrection`. When we export, we write both, to make sure we are compatible with everyone. When you import, we will only take one of the fields.

@SteveMacenski , 
The failure to load the kernel module could be due to the PC power management that interferes with the devices enumeration process. It also could be due to host-related/installation issue.

First, please verify that you have the  xHCI mode in BIOS turned on, as described in #1283.
Then inspect the enumeration process by looking into the kernel log after reboot and look for USB errors such as mentioned in #1615.  You can share the kernel log here as well.

In case the device is enumerated properly, but the kernel module is still not loaded during boot-up by Ubuntu you may enforce that by adding uvc driver to the startup list
`echo 'uvcvideo' | sudo tee -a /etc/modules`.
though I would recommend it only as the last resort.

More questions:
- Is this behavior persistent when switching USB ports on the NUC?
- Kernel 4.13.0-43 is not yet available by `sudo apt dist-upgrade` - did the PC work correctly with other (older)  kernel versions?

I found the problem finally, though it worked fine in previous versions of Django. It was how the ```urlpatterns``` were configured. I had this in my master ```urls.py```:

    urlpatterns = [
        ...
        url(r'^', include('inventory.sites.urls')),
        ]

When I changed it to:

    urlpatterns = [
        ...
        url(r'^$', include('inventory.sites.urls')),
        ]

everything worked as usual. That URL messed with the ```__debug__``` path but nothing else that came after it. I don't understand why this would have happened since the DDT path is uniquely defined.

I think we should do 3 things in response.

1. Reveal more info in the tooltips (counts for TP, FP, TN, FP, the specific threshold), so the user can reason through precision/recall calculations upon noticing suspicious areas of the chart. This effort is ongoing (#463).
2. Remove `tf.maximum(_TINY_EPISILON, tp)` from the numerator of the precision calculation (and just use `tp`). Otherwise, the user can see multiples of `_TINY_EPSILON` in the tooltip, and it's hard to decipher where that comes from. This effort is ongoing (#465).
3. On the frontend, after (1) is done (and the frontend has access to counts), we avoid rendering portions of charts for which TP+FP=0 (and precision is thus undefined). This avoids the large spike to 1 near the Y axis. A value of 1 where precision is undefined could mislead because the former implies there is area under the PR curve (but precision's actually undefined). We still render portions for which FP > 0 (and TP = 0), which could still mean a steep drop in precision to 0. If this is visually undesirable and unuseful, we can do away with that drop as well.

Hmm, I'm not sure this is quite the right solution to #884.  This has the same polymer cached HTML element re-use issues that William described here: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/issues/170#issuecomment-312325644

Specifically, in this implementation, we're using the same set of `<tf-image-loader>` elements that are inside the `<dom-repeat>` even when we switch pages.  This has surprising effects like the fact that the image's step slider setting transfers to the same `<tf-image-loader>` element on other pages (the one in the same position, that is).  However, it's actually worse than that because it's not just the slider setting that persists, but actually also the sample index requested by the loading logic when you move the slider.  So sliding the slider scrolls through images for whatever sample that slider represented when it was created; it doesn't respond to page navigation.  It seems like this affects even the initial images loaded.

While we could perhaps retool `<tf-image-loader>` to be completely stateless (i.e. everything it displays is a function of its bound properties), per what Polymer recommends, the histograms dashboard has the same issue and I'm not sure that can be retooled as easily since it's displaying a lot more data (versus just a single image at a time).  As mentioned in my internal email about the dom-repeat issues, I think we might want to look into other options.

My hunch is that you may have conflicts because you have two use cases:
1. as a library dparse (or pyup before that) uses the features of setuptools, packaging, pip to access and parse deps
2. as an installable package, dparse needs to have its own proper setup.py and be installable.

I do not think that the two can ever be resolved sanely: 

- either you aggressively pin the version of these deps that work for you and eventually make installing dparse fail in some environment that depend explicitly or implicitly on other versions of these deps for their own installation 

- or you do not pin and dparse may install but may not work if the API (or internals) of the tool in question is not what you expect.

This --using packaging tools not only for packaging, but also as libraries for package introspection-- is a very special use case (and for instance there is no API in pip as far as I know).

IMHO the only sane way is to vendor and bundle the tools to be used at runtime and use a standard setup.py for installation.

A few examples: 
- https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/tree/master/_pytest/vendored_packages
- https://github.com/pypa/pip/tree/master/pip/_vendor which without vendoring would otherwise make many users miserable ;)

@jwhui how you prefer the new TLV to be introduced? The easiest path (but least elegant) would be just have thing like:

```
#ifndef LONG_ROUTES_ENABLED
class RouteTlv { ... }
#else
class RouteTlv { ... }
#endif
```
So, rest of the code won't need updates.

Another approach would be to have

```
#ifdef LONG_ROUTES_ENABLED
class LongRouteTlv { ... }
#endif
```
and then modify each and every instance of `RouteTlv` in the code (e.g. in `FillTlv()`) with `#ifdef / #else / #endif` stuff, which I think is ugly.

Yet another approach is to introduce some base class and name it `RouteTlv` so plain old TLV can inherit from it, just as new TLV, so the only `#ifdef` stuff will be during creation of new TLV (in `FillTlv()` IIRC).

Or, maybe some other approach should be used?

> So, for Python the distinction is GAPIC vs. Proto, and yes, it does handle that distinction. You can see a separate gapic_subpath and grpc_subpath in the Artman config files for Python, and the GAPIC pipeline has code that "rolls in" the GRPC client pipeline for most languages. (I think Java is exempt).
Java is currently exempt, and it seems like it does follow a fairly different workflow here. In Java the grpc packages are updated much less often than the gapic layer (this seems like it would be the case for most languages?). This may change as the gapic layer is stabilized, although I would still expect that changes in gapic yamls will mean that the gapic layer is likely to change significantly more often than the proto/grpc packages.

Having the grpc and proto pipelines combined makes sense. But this runs into some problems using the existing taskflow setup, because the two pipelines share tasks, and some of those tasks "consume" things, like the grpc_code_dir setting. This is not to say that these problems can't be solved, but just running the tasks for these two pipelines sequentially won't work. I think that a fairly natural solution would be to allow the user to specify multiple pipelines to be run from the command line - what do you think?

I believe the problem lies in the flags you are passing to FFTW in your example.  On the first run you pass the flag 'FFTW_ESTIMATE', but then you **did not also use this flag** when passing 'FFTW_WISDOM_ONLY'.  This results in defaulting to `FFTW_MEASURE' for the effort which is a higher effort level than the previously created plan stored in your wisdom.

You would need to be passing the final FFTW call within your FFT2 function `flags=('FFTW_ESTIMATE', 'FFTW_WISDOM_ONLY')` and then it should work.  Alternatively, using 'FFTW_MEASURE' when generating the wisdom should also work.

IIRC, the rule for planner efforts is that the wisdom must contain a plan at an equal or higher level of effort for it to be used.

Assuming you mean your original post checklist, I vote for calling it `enum34` (matching the Python backport name) if it's going to be distinct from `enum_` and public.

But I vote for it being neither distinct nor public.  Rather I think that `enum_` should continue to be the single public interface, with `py3_enum` and `old_enum` (or whatever) as implementation details: `enum_` would then map to a `py3_enum` when available, falling back transparently to `old_enum` if not available.

I'm not sure it's really worth it to worry about supporting additional definitions and attributes on the bound enum.  (Or in other words, I'm perfectly happy for this to be a documented limitation, possibly to be revisited later if someone has a strong use case for it).

The `publically` one is marginally debatable, but I've no problem including it with the rest.

With pybind11, you need to register the types that occur in default arguments before you can actually use them. You can only use ``py::object`` as a default argument if that's what your method actually accepts (unlikely if it is a function from an existing codebase)

> Which hierarchy specifically? I think Python's C3 MRO supports any arbitrary acyclical hierarchy, which seem to cover all of @lyskov 's examples.

I'm referring to this example, which corresponds to the above C++ snippet:

```python
class Base1:
    pass

class Base2(Base1):
    pass

class Derived(Base1, Base2):
    pass
```

```bash

    class Derived(Base1, Base2):
TypeError: Cannot create a consistent method resolution
order (MRO) for bases Base1, Base2
```

I had to do some digging and testing here as the relevant code even predates my involvement in the project. I agree that we should do this when trying to add the cert and key directives to a virtual host.

To provide the best justification I could come up with for why things are this way, in both Apache and Nginx a virtual host like this is probably technically misconfigured. You're telling your webserver to use HTTPS but not providing a cert or key unless you've added one to your top level config which isn't done by default or a normal practice as far as I know.

This is especially true in Apache where Apache will fail to start if you tell it to use SSL/TLS without a cert and key. Nginx behaves differently and will start, but will refuse connections to the server and write messages to `error.log`. Part of the reason we do this in Nginx may be that the Nginx plugin was written to strongly mimic the way the Apache plugin worked at the time where this makes a little more sense.

With all that said, Nginx still runs in this case and the configuration is valid in either server if there are certs and keys in the top level config. I see no problem fixing this and made #4837 to track the issue in Apache.

In Nginx, this could be done as simply as catching the error thrown if `replace=True` and the directive doesn't exist and calling again with `replace=False`. We could make an additional function in `parser.py` to do this if we wanted. Alternatively, we could just make `replace=True` have this behavior by default, but I'd carefully audit the code to make sure this is what we want to happen in every case.

I was able to fix the problem on Mac OS X 10.9.5 with Server 3.2.2 without modifying auto generated configuration files.

Create a web app configuration that disables a reverse proxy for ```/.well-known/acme-challenge``` and save it as ```/Library/Server/Web/Config/apache2/webapps/org.letsencrypt.acme.plist```
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<!-- save as /Library/Server/Web/Config/apache2/webapps/org.letsencrypt.acme.plist -->
<!-- See man pages for webapp.plist(5) and webappctl(8) for information about web app configuration -->
<plist version="1.0">
<dict> 
	<key>name</key>
	<string>org.letsencrypt.acme</string>
	<key>displayName</key>		<!-- Name shown in Server app -->
	<string>Let's Encrypt ACME</string>
	<key>proxies</key>		<!-- ProxyPass/ProxyPassReverse directives are activated when webapp is started -->
	<dict>
		<key>/.well-known/acme-challenge</key>		<!-- Sets up a reverse proxy -->
		<dict>
			<key>path</key>
			<string>/.well-known/acme-challenge</string>
			<key>urls</key>
			<array/> <!-- Disable Proxy --> 
		</dict>
	</dict>
	<key>sslPolicy</key>	<!-- Determines webapp SSL behavior -->
	<integer>0</integer>	<!-- 0: default, UseSSLWhenEnabled -->
			<!-- 1:	UseSSLAlways -->
			<!-- 2:	UseSSLOnlyWhenCertificateIsTrustable -->
			<!-- 3:	UseSSLNever -->
			<!-- 4:	UseSSLAndNonSSL -->
</dict>
</plist>
```
Start the "web app" with ```sudo webappctl start org.letsencrypt.acme```.
Check that ```ProxyPass /.well-known/acme-challenge !``` is before the the other ```ProxyPass``` lines in your config  (```/Library/Server/Web/Config/apache2/sites/0000_any_443_.conf```).

If that's not the case you might need to restart the calendar and addressbook server to get the order right.
```sh
sudo serveradmin stop calendar
sudo serveradmin stop addressbook
sudo serveradmin start addressbook
sudo serveradmin start calendar
```

Now you should be able to use ```letsencrypt``` with automatic ACME challenges.
**Note:** The above file paths might be different on newer systems.

Notes about msgpack:

####  Does the format support native upgrades?

No. Must be done manually. Could use the same approach as avro's single object encoding.

#### Does renaming break compatibility?

No, the name is not part of the serialized format.

#### Does it support the 256 bits types?

No, but it supports application defined types

#### Changing fields types?

This changes the encoding and would break without a version check.

#### Adding fields?

Must be done manually

I think the problem here is that

1. SoS submits the job to the queue, change the status to `submitted`. This state is marked by the existence and the execution of a `.sh` file, and the job is not yet running.
2. SoS expects the status changed to
  * `running` if the pulse thread starts from `sos execute`.
  * then `failed` if the pulse file exists but sos quits with a `.res` file with error code.
  * then `aborted` if the pulse file stops.
3. However, your `slurm` script seemed failed without executing the `sos` command, so it fails even before a pulse thread is started, so to the eyes of sos nothing has changed compared to the `submitted` status.

task: inline=True

In this code, the tasks would be executed inside of SoS. They would not have task id and all the status stuff but will be executed in parallel.

I think we should avoid using [`scipy.misc.imresize` as it is deprecated](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/misc/pilutil.py#L509-L512). Furthermore, internally it converts to [a PIL image anyway](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/misc/pilutil.py#L554).


If the `ToTensor`/`ToPILImage` is not yet working as you need, I think we should instead fix `ToPILImage`/`ToTensor` to handle the missing cases, but I'd prefer to keep using PIL images for the moment, as it stores more information than numpy arrays (colorspace for e.g.).

Maybe a possibility is to get inspiration from [`toimage` from scipy to complement](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/misc/pilutil.py#L288) `ToPILImage`?

I took a 3-pronged attack here:

1. improved tree-shaking so that we don't try to serialize out things we don't need.  This is super useful since we commonly have  ```new module1.module2.Type```, and we really do not want to capture/serialize `module1` or `module2`. (Previously the optimization only did this for the first name in the dotted chain.)
2. I marked certain functions that cannot be serialized as `doNotCapture`.  This way if something does end up pulling these in unintentionally, they do not break closure serialization.
3. I improved how `doNotCapture` functions are serialized.  Previously, we just ignored them entirely.  Now we serialize them over as a function that throws, telling you what the problem is more clearly.

This description sounds good to me from the Dask perspective.  I think that the centralized vs bottom-up/distributed scheduling is maybe the central difference.  

The question I'm now curious about is "is there anything that Dask should learn and copy from Ray?"  I hope that this question comes across more as flattering than as encroaching :)  For example, I suspect that we could copy something like the actor model API decently easily.  We do something already with long-running clients on workers (here is a simple script that includes [an example](https://gist.github.com/mrocklin/a92785743744b5c698984e16b7065037)) but it could be that this isn't the way that people want to think about these sorts of problems.

There are likely some core differences that Dask will probably never be able to support, but I'd be very open to seeing if there are opportunities or advantages for collaboration in some settings.

No, the documentation is correct. 

> Fine tune best/worst synonyms by excluding unwanted streams.

`--stream-sorting-excludes` doesn't filter the available qualities, but instead is being used for filtering the `best` and `worst` quality aliases. If you're using this parameter, you need to use the `best` or `worst` quality selector to get the matched streams.

And since Twitch is using different quality formats (by resolution, by resolution+framerate and by name), you need to always specify the framerate in the explicit formats and also select the old quality names in addition to that.

See here:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/828#issuecomment-297419286

The `--default-stream` parameter behaves exactly the same the the normal quality selection after the stream URL. It's just there for being able to define default quality selections in the config file.

> Either way, it would be good to write a test that everything in the lower-level modules `__all__` is exported at the top level.

I'd be tempted to get rid of the lower-level `__all__`'s too. If we're not using them, then what's the point of maintaining and testing them?

I guess the case where this test would catch a bug is: someone adds a new symbol, and remembers to add it to the module's `__all__`, but forgets to add it to the higher-level `import` line – AND if we didn't have the `__all__` then they would have forgotten to export it anywhere. I guess this scenario could happen? We all forget stuff sometimes. But I feel like if someone's going to forget to export something, then it doesn't help to have *two* places they're supposed to export it.

Wow, that's super clever. I like that it's much simpler than the current version of this hack, and that it can be motivated as an invisible optimization. (Maybe we wouldn't even want to document that `yield_from_` works in native async generators.)

I suspect you'll run into a bit of trouble getting that to pass the current test suite, because we have some tests to check that we don't suffer from [bpo-32526](https://bugs.python.org/issue32526), and native async generators *do* suffer from that bug. Someone should probably fix that at some point... But I guess merely working as well as native async generators would be OK, and we could mark that test as `xfail` for now (on configurations where the hack is used).

I think this will also cause behavioral differences with respect to the async generator GC hooks. Currently `@async_generator` functions never interact with those hooks (unlike native async generators), and I think with your hack it will start using the GC hooks. This is arguably the right thing. There's some discussion in #13. It's definitely complicated enough that we'd want to add tests for it though! And once PyPy gets native async generator support then we'll probably need to also implement GC hook support inside `AsyncGenerator`, since it won't be using this hack.

I tried to dig into this a little this evening without a lot of success. The assertion is being thrown because the poller still has one file descriptor that wasn't removed (i.e., `add_fd` was called one time more than `rm_fd`). On a successful run (with `ctx.term()`), the `kqueue_t` destructor joins what I believe is the context's IO thread, and before that join returns the IO thread removes the final fd. On a failed run, the join returns immediately as though the IO thread had died somehow without ever removing one of its fds.
